transformative habit that clears the mental clutter and gives me control over my day first I begin with the brain dump I journal every thought task and worry that's cluttering my mind research in cognitive psychology shows that this kind of externalization reduces mental load and decision fatigue allowing your brain to relax and focus better later on it's like clearing up the Clutter on your •	Brain Juice (03:17 - 04:19): She emphasizes that exercise is non-negotiable, noting that it boosts Brain-Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF), which enhances memory, focus, and executive function.

•	Matcha Before Mayhem (07:30 - 08:39): Faye opts for matcha over coffee, explaining that the L-theanine content provides a calm, focused alertness without the typical jitters or energy crashes.
•	Midday Reset (08:40 - 13:15): She incorporates mindfulness and meditation to combat midday energy dips and lower cortisol levels, helping her stay present during stressful or high-stakes moments.
•	Push & Rest (13:16 - 13:59): By tackling her most important tasks early via the Eisenhower Matrix, she can fully 'switch off' in the evening, establishing firm work boundaries to improve sleep and reduce rumination.
•	Quality Time (14:00 - 14:45): Ending the day with focused, meaningful social interaction to boost oxytocin and reconnect with what matters most.

So that by the time you know that you're 16, 17, 18, you're not going to be fat. cuz it' be very rare that you're going to see a a child that's fat in Japan. I think that carries you your whole life. It sets your diet. You got like a nutrition education that actually worked. Yeah. The fourth key is portion sizes. The portion sizes are generally just a little smaller wherever you go. Normal size, like meal size in Australia is usually big. I couldn't finish eating them, but then I used got used to it and I actually gained 12 kg there. So that's like 25 lbs. M double tacos designed to keep your stomach and your wallet full. I'm full. America's biggest breakfast for only $9.99. I think there's two big reasons why Japan doesn't take the American approach of giving you huge portions to make you feel like you got your money's worth. First off, don't do this in Japan. A big concept here is multi or wasteful. Uh elementary school, middle school, if you cannot finish the meal, you don't have like lunch break. So, you just have to sit there. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Like like I remember some of my friends couldn't finish eating and then like crying and eating. The idea is came from like you cannot waste the food because that's the life of plants and animals. Yeah, leaving food is considered bad. It's kind of bad manners to leave unfinished food and especially rice scattered about on your plate. Next, it's not really a thing here to get a container to take leftovers home. You're expected to finish your meal at the restaurant. So, the average restaurant serves people reasonably sized, not too big portions that most people should be able to finish in one sitting. Most packages of snacks here are sized so that you can comfortably finish them without feeling stuffed. Like, look how small all these little packages of chocolates are. If you eat the normal American diet, you'll get fat. In Japan, if you just eat what everyone else eats, then you get thin. Yeah. Do you think that's pretty accurate? What I like about drinking culture in Japan is that even if you keep drinking at is both you there you see so many menu the healthy food on menu if you go to America fried chicken you know fried potato you know like French fries most of the places to go to drink have decent food options you can expect to get some really solid food that's actually quite healthy at a classic Japanese pub in izakaya there are about 7500 izakayas in Japan more than the total number of fast food burger chains like McDonald's or Burger King. Now, I'm not necessarily saying you should replace fast food with alcohol. But even if you're walking in somewhere planning to get drunk, there's all kinds of simple, healthy, and delicious dishes. There's one other quick point I want to touch on, but first, let me make it clear that there are better quality food options than convenience stores, rice bowl chains, or isukai. Of course, most Japanese people wouldn't even think of these places as healthy. But this isn't about optimal health. It's about how you can be reasonably healthy with not that much effort. Uh, did you make this with vegetable oil? Oh, yes. Thanks, but I got to watch what I eat. Lately, the word has been getting out about how bad vegetable oils or seed oils are for health. It's kind of impressive how clever marketing got us to think that we need to crush and heavily refine thousands of seeds with a long industrial process to get the oil that we need to cook with to be healthy with high pressure forcing out the oil to a second extraction. 70-minute wash with a solvent. They wash the oil with sodium hydroxide. They bleach it, then use a steam injection heating process to remove the canola odor. Sure, you can buy vegetable oil in Japan, and people do cook with it, but it's very interesting to note that Japanese people consume about 1/ half of the seed oils of Americans. The ironic thing about seed oils is that they become more unhealthy when you cook with them, especially if you fry with them. That's because the fats are very fragile and easily break down when heated, which is not the case for saturated fats like say beef tallow, which are very resilient to heat. There is a place for stuff like protein shakes and bars, but someone like me definitely doesn't need that dopamine spike on a daily basis from those delicious high protein snacks. That sweet taste would just set me off on a roller coaster of poor choices. Ultimately, this comes down to me wanting and functioning so much better on real food. Nutrientdense food is the key. So, what have been the results of eating a minimum of 150 g of protein a day? The magic thing about eating high protein, it fills me up and it stops the constant food noise that I used to have. So, no more cravings. And by the time I've eaten the protein, then there's not as much room in my body for the ultrarocessed foods, which then has its own benefits. And because protein is filling, it's easier for me to eat within my calorie range, so I can stay on top of my body fat with not much effort at all. I've been weight training and eating high protein meals for just over 2 years. Before doing this 150 g experiment, I was eating about 120 to 140 g of protein a day. But since increasing to 150, 160, 170 g a day, the results have been remarkable. My muscle mass and strength have increased significantly. I reached a plateau with my exercise and stayed there for months and couldn't break through. But with the extra protein, I broke through that plateau. I will say for transparency, at the same time as increasing the protein, I also increased the amount of creatine that I'm taking, going from one dose a day to three doses a day. That likely made a difference to my strength and muscle mass, too. but not all of what I've experienced from doing this. With more muscle, I'm able to work harder and generate more output during my interval training sessions, which is then allowing me to increase my V2 max. And a high V2 max is associated with living a longer and healthier life. Each time I get my haircut, my hairdresser tells me that my hair is growing thicker and faster than before. And I've actually been able to grow my hair longer over the past few months. And I haven't done that before because my hair was so thin and wispy. My blood sugar is stable throughout the day because of the high protein and vegetables. As a result, I have sustained energy, which then leads to better concentration, which for someone like me is an absolute bonus. And this is a good bit. This is what I looked like when I was eating 120 to 140 g of protein a day. It was a vast improvement from me being skinny fat 2 years previous. And this is what I look like now on 150 to 170 grams of protein a day. There's obviously another nine months of weight training in this picture, but I'm much stronger now. And you know what? I feel really good. Do you know how much protein you eat in a day? What do you think about this 150 g plan? I'd love to hear from you in the comments. - Over 60? The Best COLLAGEN-RICH VEGETABLES You MUST EAT Every Day | Senior Women’s Health: Kind: captions Language: en Most women over 60 believe their stiff knees, weak legs, or tingling hands are simply the price of aging. Yet, what few are told is that these changes are often linked to a silent breakdown in the body's collagen system, long before pain becomes obvious. What's even more surprising is that this process is not corrected by supplements or treatments alone, but by specific vegetables that send daily biological signals your body depends on to stay strong. I'm Dr. Eliza Hart, a boardcertified obstitrician and gynecologist with over 15 years of experience supporting women through hormonal and structural changes that occur after midlife. And one of the most common misunderstandings I see is the belief that collagen loss after 60 is only about skin or appearance. In reality, collagen is the internal framework that supports your joints, cushions your nerves, stabilizes your muscles, and helps you move with confidence. Which is why its decline often shows up as stiffness, weakness, or tingling rather than wrinkles alone. The encouraging truth is that even after 60, your body has not lost its ability to rebuild collagen. But it does require the right daily signals rather than quick fixes or expensive promises. Before we begin, I invite you to support this channel by subscribing. Now, let's begin with the vegetables that quietly help your body rebuild strength from within. One, red bell peppers, the collagen on switch. Before collagen can be rebuilt in any meaningful way, after 60, the body first needs a clear biological signal that tells it repair is both possible and safe. And this is where red bell peppers quietly play a foundational role. Collagen does not begin with collagen itself, but with vitamin C, nutrient that activates the enzymes responsible for assembling amino acids into strong, flexible collagen fibers. As women age, this assembly process becomes less efficient, meaning collagen production may stall even when protein intake appears adequate. Without enough vitamin C, collagen repair remains incomplete, leaving joints more vulnerable nerves less cushioned and recovery from daily movement noticeably slower. What makes red bell peppers especially valuable is that their vitamin C content not only supports new collagen formation, but also helps protect existing collagen from oxidative stress, particularly in high use areas such as the knees, wrists, ankles, and fingers. Clinical research consistently shows that older adults with higher vitamin C intake demonstrate better grip strength, improved mobility, and lower inflammatory markers outcomes that reflect healthier connective tissue rather than cosmetic change. To benefit fully, however, how you use red bell peppers matters just as much as eating them. After 60, the goal is consistency rather than volume. And half a cup per day is more than sufficient to provide the signal your body needs. Red bell peppers are best eaten raw, lightly sliced into salads, blended into spreads, or added at the very end of cooking because prolonged heat quickly destroys vitamin C. One of the most common mistakes I see is overcooking peppers or relying on jarred, roasted, or heavily processed versions, which offer color and flavor but little collagen support. Another mistake is assuming more is better when excessive portions provide no added benefit and may cause digestive discomfort. Used gently and regularly, red bell peppers act as the quiet switch that prepares your body for deeper repair, creating the conditions collagen needs before any further support can truly work. Two, spinach, the collagen protector. If collagen were a structure under repair, spinach would be the material that keeps it from collapsing before the work is finished. And this is why many women notice stiffness returning even when they believe they are doing everything right. After 60, the issue is often not a lack of collagen signals, but an internal environment that quietly breaks collagen down as fast as the body tries to rebuild it. Spinach plays a critical role here because it does not initiate repair but protects the collagen framework from inflammation, poor circulation and mineral depletion that accelerate decline. From a medical standpoint, spinach supports collagen stability through several interconnected mechanisms. It provides vitamin A in the form of beta carotene which regulates fibrolast activity, the cells responsible for producing and maintaining collagen fibers. As estrogen levels fall after menopause, fibroblasts become less responsive, leading to thinner connective tissue and slower repair. Spinach also supplies magnesium, a mineral required for collagen related enzyme function, muscle relaxation, and healthy nerve signaling. Magnesium deficiency, which affects a large percentage of women over 60, often shows up as muscle tightness, nighttime cramps, tingling hands, and reduce circulation. In addition, spinach contains natural nitrates that improve blood flow, allowing oxygen and nutrients to reach collagen rich areas such as the knees, calves, hands, and feet. To use spinach effectively, gentle preparation is essential. One loosely packed cup per serving, lightly steamed or sauteed for one to two minutes, improves mineral absorption while preserving its protective compounds. Adding a small amount of olive oil enhances absorption of fat soluble nutrients. A common mistake is relying on large raw spinach salads daily, which can interfere with mineral uptake and irritate digestion over time. Another is overcooking spinach until it becomes watery and dark, stripping away much of its benefit. When used consistently and thoughtfully, spinach acts as a quiet stabilizer, helping the collagen your body rebuilds remain intact as we move toward deeper joint and cartilage support. Three, okra joint lubrication and cartilage support. There is a very specific moment many women recognize after 60 when standing up from a chair feels harder than it should. The knees resist the first few steps or the fingers seem to hesitate before fully opening. And this sensation is often blamed on bone aging without looking deeper. In reality, what frequently disappears first is not bone, but the smooth internal cushioning that allows joints to move quietly and comfortably. Okra becomes especially important at this stage because it addresses something collagen alone cannot fix, which is the loss of natural lubrication and protection inside aging joints. Medically, this change happens as collagen thins and synenovial fluid, the substance that allows joints to glide without friction, gradually decreases. Okra is rich in mucilage, a gel-like soluble fiber that closely mimics the body's own joint lubrication, creating a more favorable environment for cartilage and connective tissue to recover. It also provides vitamin C to support collagen synthesis, vitamin K, to help guide calcium away from soft tissues and manganesees, a trace mineral involved in forming proline, one of collagen's essential building blocks. Together, these nutrients help reduce friction, calm low-grade inflammation, and protect cartilage surfaces that would otherwise continue to deteriorate. Okra's antioxidant compounds further support nerve protection, which is why stiffness and tingling often appear together when joint support declines. Using okra correctly, makes a significant difference. One half to one cup per serving, prepared gently, is enough to support joint comfort without overwhelming digestion. Light steaming, sautéing, or adding okra to soups preserves its mucelage while making it easier to tolerate. Pairing okra with healthy fats such as olive oil improves absorption of fat soluble nutrients. A common mistake is overcooking okra until it becomes overly slimy or mushy, which reduces its functional benefit. while another is avoiding it entirely because of texture without experimenting with preparation methods. When introduced gradually and used consistently, okra helps restore the quiet smooth movement many women miss preparing the joints for deeper structural support in the sections ahead. Four, broccoli fibroblast protector. Many women focus on feeding collagen after 60. Yet few realize that collagen repair can quietly fail if the very cells responsible for producing it are under constant attack. This is why joints may continue to feel unstable, tendons less responsive, and movement less confident even when nutrition appears adequate. Broccoli enters the picture not as a builder but as a cellular shield protecting the collagen producing machinery itself at a stage when those cells become increasingly vulnerable. From a medical perspective the key lies in fibroblasts the specialized cells that manufacture and maintain collagen fibers with age. Chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, and blood sugar fluctuations weaken fibroblast function, causing collagen to break down faster than it can be replaced. Broccoli contains sulforophane, a compound known to activate the body's internal repair and detoxification pathways, helping fibroblasts resist inflammatory damage and maintain their ability to produce stable connective tissue. Broccoli also provides vitamin C to support collagen assembly. vitamin K to ensure calcium is directed into bones rather than accumulating in joints and a range of antioxidants that reduce glycation, a process that stiffens collagen and makes it brittle. This combination is particularly important for women experiencing cracking knees, unstable ankles, or a deep dull ache that seems to come from within the joint rather than the surface. How broccoli is prepared often determines whether it supports or undermines these benefits. Light steaming for 2 to 3 minutes is ideal as it preserves sulfurophane while improving digestibility. One cup per serving several times per week is sufficient to support fibroblast protection without placing stress on digestion. Adding a small amount of olive oil enhances absorption of fat soluble compounds. A common mistake is boiling broccoli until it becomes soft and pale, which strips away much of its protective value, while another is avoiding it due to bloating without adjusting portion size or cooking method. When used thoughtfully, broccoli helps safeguard the cells that keep collagen renewal possible, creating a stronger internal foundation before circulation focused support becomes the next priority. Five beats circulation and collagen delivery. There is a quiet frustration many women feel after 60 when they try to do everything right for their joints and still notice heaviness in the legs, cold feet, or a lingering weakness that appears after even short walks. And this often signals a problem that nutrition alone cannot solve without adequate circulation. Collagen repair does not happen in isolation because the cells responsible for rebuilding connective tissue depend on a steady supply of oxygen minerals and signaling nutrients carried by healthy blood flow. Beets become important at this stage because they address the delivery system ensuring that the collagen support you provide can actually reach the tissues that need it most. Medically, beets are rich in natural nitrates, which the body converts into nitric oxide, a compound that relaxes blood vessels and improves circulation. As circulation declines with age, collagen producing cells in the legs, knees, feet, and hands often receive fewer resources, slowing repair and increasing stiffness. Improved nitric oxide activity has been shown to enhance lower limb blood flow, walking endurance, and muscle activation while indirectly supporting collagen renewal by restoring access to nutrients. Beets also contain betylain's powerful antioxidants that protect collagen from oxidative stress and folate, which helps regulate homocyine levels, a compound linked to vascular stiffness and connective tissue damage when elevated. Using beets correctly is essential to avoid common mistakes. 1/2 to one cup per serving several times per week is enough to support circulation without overwhelming blood sugar balance. Light steaming or roasting preserves their benefits better than boiling, which causes nutrients to leech into the water. Fresh unsweetened beet juice can also be used in small portions, ideally paired with vitamin C rich vegetables to enhance nitric oxide activity. A frequent error is consuming large amounts daily or combining beets with sugary additives which can counteract their benefits. If circulation has been a challenge for you, comment one. And if you feel blood flow has never been an issue, comment zero so I can better understand where this support matters most. When used thoughtfully, beets help reopen the pathways collagen depends on preparing the body for mineral-based repair in the next step. Six, seaweed, trace minerals for strong collagen. It often comes as a surprise that even with good protein intake and careful vegetable choices, collagen repair can still feel incomplete, as if the body knows what to do, but lacks the final pieces to finish the job. This is because collagen is not built by vitamins alone. And after 60, the smallest missing elements can determine whether new collagen fibers become strong and resilient or weak and disorganized. Seaweed enters at this precise point, supplying the trace minerals that quietly decide the quality of the collagen your body produces. From a medical standpoint, collagen assembly depends on minerals such as iodine, zinc, copper, and manganese, which act as enzyme activators during collagen formation. Seaweed provides these minerals in highly bioavailable forms, allowing fibroblast to produce collagen with proper structure rather than fragile strands that break down quickly. One compound unique to many edible seaweeds is fukcoidin, a natural polysaccharide shown to stimulate fibroblast activity and reduce inflammation around joints and nerve pathways. Seaweed also contains algenate fiber that helps calm inflammatory stress within connective tissue, giving collagen a more stable environment to rebuild. Another often overlooked role is iodine support of thyroid function, which influences metabolic rate and tissue repair, speed factors that commonly slow after menopause and contribute to cold hands, weak legs, and delayed recovery. Using seaweed correctly requires moderation and attention to quality. Two to three small servings per week are sufficient because trace minerals work in tiny amounts and excess offers no added benefit. Dried nori sheets, plain seaweed flakes, or low sodium seaweed snacks are preferable choices. Seaweed can be crumbled into soups, sprinkled over salads, or added to rice dishes without dominating flavor. A common mistake is choosing heavily salted or flavored seaweed products, which may strain blood pressure or digestion. Another is assuming more frequent use will accelerate results. When introduced gently and consistently, seaweed supplies the missing mineral signals that allow collagen to be built with strength, preparing the body for the next layer of internal protection. Seven, asparagus, glutathione, and collagen shield. There is a point after 60 when women notice that even careful nutrition seems to lose its protective effect as if the body repairs during the day but quietly unravels again overnight leaving joints aching and nerves more sensitive by morning. This pattern is rarely about effort or consistency, but about protection. Because collagen that is newly formed can still be broken down faster than it is rebuilt if oxidative stress remains unchecked. Asparagus becomes important here because it strengthens the body's internal defense system, allowing collagen to survive long enough to do its work. From a medical perspective, asparagus is one of the richest natural sources of glutathione. Often described as the body's master antioxidant, glutathione neutralizes free radicals that attack collagen fibers in joints, tendons, skin, and the protective sheets surrounding nerves. After menopause, glutathione production commonly declines, leaving collagen more fragile, and tissues more vulnerable to inflammation and breakdown. This is why joint discomfort, skin thinning, and nighttime tingling often appear together. Asparagus also provides vitamin K, which plays a crucial role in directing calcium into bones rather than allowing it to accumulate in soft tissues where it can stiffen collagen and irritate nearby nerves. In addition, asparagus supplies folate, a nutrient essential for cell division and tissue renewal, supporting the steady replacement of aging collagen producing cells. To use asparagus effectively, gentle cooking and portion awareness are key. Lightly steaming or sautéing asparagus until just tender preserves its antioxidant capacity while improving digestion, making about one cup per serving ideal. Pairing it with a small amount of olive oil enhances absorption of fat soluble nutrients while including it several times per week supports ongoing protection rather than short-term effects. A common mistake is over cooking asparagus until it becomes limp and dull, which significantly reduces its protective value. Another is overlooking it entirely because its benefits feel subtle rather than dramatic. When used consistently, asparagus acts as a quiet shield, helping the collagen your body rebuilds remain intact, resilient, and protected as we move toward the final stage of daily renewal. Eight, parsley, the daily collagen rebuilder. What often separates steady improvement from ongoing decline after 60 is not intensity, but the presence of a small daily signal that the body can recognize and respond to consistently. And this is where parsley quietly outperforms many foods that receive far more attention. Often treated as a garnish and pushed aside, parsley carries an unusually concentrated combination of nutrients that speak directly to the collagen system, making it one of the most efficient daily tools for rebuilding and preserving connective tissue. From a medical standpoint, parsley stands out because of its exceptionally high vitamin C content in a very small volume, which is critical for assembling new collagen fibers day after day without overwhelming digestion. Unlike larger servings of fruit, parsley delivers this signal without raising blood sugar, an important consideration after menopause when glucose fluctuations can accelerate collagen breakdown. Parsley is also rich in vitamin K, a nutrient essential for keeping collagenrich tissues flexible by ensuring calcium is directed into bones rather than deposited in joints, cartilage, and blood vessels where it contributes to stiffness and nerve irritation. In addition, parsley contains apagenin, a flavonoid shown to protect fibroblasts from inflammatory stress, allowing newly formed collagen to remain intact instead of being rapidly degraded. Its natural support of glutathione production further strengthens the body's antioxidant defenses, slowing the silent erosion of connective tissue that often accompanies aging. Using parsley effectively is about simplicity and repetition rather than large portions. 2 to 3 tablespoons of fresh, finely chopped parsley daily is sufficient to provide meaningful support. It can be added to eggs, soups, salads, smoothies, or sprinkled over cooked vegetables without altering meals significantly. A common mistake is treating parsley as an occasional decoration rather than a daily ingredient, while another is relying solely on dried parsley, which offers reduced potency compared to fresh. When used consistently, parsley acts as a quiet daily reminder to the body to rebuild, protect, and maintain the collagen framework that supports confident movement as we approach the final step of practical integration. Nine, how to use these vegetables safely and effectively after 60. At this point, many women begin to wonder whether real progress requires eating all of these vegetables every day or following a rigid plan that feels overwhelming. And this concern alone often becomes the reason nothing changes. The truth is that collagen repair after 60 responds far better to consistency and rhythm than to intensity and understanding. How to apply what you've learned is just as important as knowing which vegetables matter most. From a clinical perspective, the body adapts best when it receives clear, repeated signals without being pushed into stress. You do not need all eight vegetables daily because collagen responds to patterns rather than volume. Choosing two or three vegetables at a time allows your digestive system, circulation, and connective tissue to adjust gradually, which is especially important after menopause when sensitivity to dietary changes increases. Rotating vegetables across the week provides a broader range of nutrients while reducing the risk of excess fiber, mineral imbalance, or digestive discomfort. This gentle approach supports steady collagen rebuilding without triggering inflammation or fatigue. In practice, the most effective method is to begin with one foundation vegetable and one supportive vegetable for the first week using moderate portions once per day. During the second and third weeks, you can introduce variety by rotating options based on availability and preference. Light cooking methods such as steaming quick sautéing or adding vegetables at the end of cooking help preserve nutrients while improving tolerance. Pairing vegetables with healthy fats like olive oil enhances absorption of fat soluble vitamins and reduces gastrointestinal strain. Common mistakes include trying to overhaul the diet all at once, eating excessive raw vegetables that are harder to digest, or assuming that larger portions will accelerate results. Progress after 60 is subtle. At first, often showing up as easier movement, reduced morning stiffness, or calmer nerves. When these vegetables are used thoughtfully and patiently, they work together as a system, preparing your body for long-term strength rather than short-lived improvement. As we've seen, collagen loss after 60 is not a sudden failure of the body, but a gradual shift that responds best to steady, thoughtful support rather than force or urgency. Each vegetable we discussed plays a different role from activating collagen repair to protecting it, delivering it, and helping it last. Then, you are going to place the bowl on the&nbsp; scale, and set it to zero with the sifter on top. And I'm going to add 2g of matcha. This process is going to break up the matcha&nbsp; powder and give you a much nicer texture. Then finally, add hot water. I tested it many times, then I found that&nbsp;&nbsp; 90g of hot water at 65 ℃ (149 ° F)&nbsp; is the best for traditional matcha. Then, you want to whisk for&nbsp; 15 seconds with a chasen. When whisking, it’s preferable to move it&nbsp; like writing M instead of making circles. And I do about 3 seconds of gathering in. Then I do 5 seconds of vigorous whisking. And about 6 seconds you’ll&nbsp; see I’ve started moving slower&nbsp;&nbsp; and then gently mix the surface&nbsp; to get really nice foam. Finally, put the chasen center and pull&nbsp; it out of the middle of the matcha. You want to check the foam attached&nbsp; to the tip of the chasen like this,&nbsp;&nbsp; that’s the sign that you got a nice froth. It's complete. Next is the method for those who don’t&nbsp; have the normal tools to make such matcha. This is what you need to prepare. After warming up the Mason jar, put&nbsp; matcha and hot water in the same way... then... Shake it!!! for 15 seconds!! And it's complete. Pretty easy, isn’t it? I think matcha made with chasen&nbsp; is more delicious of course,&nbsp;&nbsp; but this method also gets the job done. &nbsp; By the way, today I used this&nbsp; brand of matcha called Matcha Moon. And this gave a good texture and flavorful taste. Also, in the future, I think I’ll&nbsp; make a matcha comparison video. During the morning Today I was making a kamakura,&nbsp; which is an igloo with my friend. 12:00 pm Lunch Now it's time for lunch. These days I’m really into Mentai Kamatama Udon. Kamatama-Udon is boiled udon&nbsp; noodles with raw egg and soy sauce. I always put mentaiko in it as&nbsp; well, which is spicy cod roe. When the noodles are boiled, I strain&nbsp; them and then put them in a bowl. Add mentaiko, an egg, Then pour soy sauce and Yuzupon on it. That’s it. Super simple! Then, I mix it up and eat it. I love it so much that I never&nbsp; get tired of eating it every day. By the way, apparently mentaiko is sold under the&nbsp; name, “Hakata spicy caviar”, outside of Japan. During the Afternoon Finally, the kamakura is completed. I enjoyed drinking matcha with my friend. I will also make a video about this&nbsp; too, so please look forward to it. 18:30 pm Dinner Well, I’m going to make dinner. First, I cook rice in a clay pot. Then put pesticide-free brown&nbsp; rice in the pot and add water. Actually, you want to soak the brown rice in water&nbsp; for about 30 minutes in advance before cooking,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you want to make more delicious rice. But I was so hungry that I couldn’t wait. haha I cook it on the electric stove&nbsp; for about 20 minutes on high heat. If you use a gas stove, 15 minutes&nbsp; is about enough on high medium heat. After that, I turn off the heat&nbsp; and leave it for 20 minutes. Make sure not to open the lid. I’m going to prepare side&nbsp; dishes while the rice cooks. Today, I’m gonna grill saury on&nbsp; the fish grill, under the stove. Reheat the miso soup I made yesterday. The ingredients for miso soup are&nbsp; tofu, nameko (which are mushrooms),&nbsp;&nbsp; green onions, and white radishes. This is the miso I used,&nbsp; which is a 3-year-old miso&nbsp;&nbsp; made and purchased in the hot spring&nbsp; town near the Snow Monkey Park. By the way, I am currently&nbsp; using a Teflon-coated pot,&nbsp;&nbsp; but I would like to buy a cast-iron&nbsp; pot in the future. While the rice finished&nbsp; cooking, I grated the radish. Mmmm it looks delicious! I'm a minimalist, so I have only these plates. I’m going to put the food on a plate, take out&nbsp; chopsticks, then arrange them on the table. Ok! it’s finally time to eat. Itadakimasu. After that, I made matcha in&nbsp; the kamakura again at night&nbsp;&nbsp; because it was very beautiful.&nbsp; It was cold, but fun. Summary Today, I introduced what I eat&nbsp; I'm sorry it's not very fancy and&nbsp; cool, but I promise it’s delicious! In the future,&nbsp;&nbsp; I hope my meals will help inspire you to&nbsp; create even more delicious and healthy dishes. However, if you think this is something&nbsp; that you want to incorporate into your life,&nbsp;&nbsp; I think that’s really great. - Top 10 Morning Foods You Should Eat Every Day: Kind: captions Language: en-US Top 10 Morning Foods You Should Eat Every Day Breakfast is the most important meal of the day as what you eat in the morning can affect your entire day. A study published in the International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health shows that eating a healthy breakfast is linked with better nutrient intake and improved overall health. But all breakfasts are not created equal. The quality matters a lot. So what should you eat in the morning to stay healthy and energetic all day? Keep watching. In today's video, we will share with you the top 10 morning foods that are easy to prepare and are good for your health. #1. Eggs Want to start your morning with something simple and nutritious? Eggs are the best option. They are full of protein and healthy fats which keep you full for a longer time and help in weight management while giving you sufficient energy. A study published in the Journal of American College of Nutrition shows that when eggs are eaten at breakfast, the total amount of daily calories consumed decreases. Additionally, egg yolk contains antioxidants, lutein, and zeaxanthin, which prevent eye disorders and improve vision. Eggs are also one of the best sources of choline, an essential nutrient that stimulates brain development and improves liver function. Prepare omelet, scrambled eggs, or boil them, whatever the way you like, and pair them with whole-grain toast or sautéed vegetables, and your delicious breakfast is ready. #2. Oatmeal It is rich in carbohydrates, fiber, protein, vitamins, and minerals, making it a perfect morning food. With just one cup of oatmeal, you get 13 grams of protein, 8 grams of fiber, and minerals like iron, folate, zinc, and phosphorus, essential for normal body functioning. A study published in the journal Nutritional Reviews shows that beta-glucan, a type of fiber present in oats, reduces LDL (low-density Lipo-protein) cholesterol and lowers the risk of cardiovascular diseases. Plus, it also promotes a feeling of fullness and prevents overeating, helping you maintain your overall calorie intake. For making it more nutritious, add a splash of dairy or soy milk and almond butter. Just stay away from the ones containing artificial flavoring and added sugar. #3. Flaxseed Despite their tiny size, flaxseeds are an excellent source of many essential nutrients, including omega-3 fatty acids. It is an essential fat that lowers LDL or bad cholesterol levels and reduces inflammation and damage, which otherwise could lead to chronic diseases like diabetes, heart diseases, and arthritis. The list of flaxseed benefits does not end with omega-3 fatty acids; it is also a great source of fiber, protein, antioxidants, and minerals like magnesium and selenium. Are you thinking about how to add them to your diet? Blend them into your morning smoothie or as a topping to oatmeal. If you want weight loss benefits, try it with yogurt. This powerful combination will boost your metabolism and keep you healthy and fit. #4. Berries How about starting your day with something sweet and healthy? Eating berries is a perfect way to do that. They are good in taste, have beautiful colors, and are brimming with health benefits. A study published in the journal Circulation shows that a higher intake of antioxidants present in berries is associated with a lower risk of heart diseases. They are also rich in fiber which helps you stay full until lunch and lower your overall caloric intake. You can add them to your smoothie, yogurt, and oatmeal or enjoy them as they taste delicious in every way. And if you are going for frozen berries, make sure they do not contain any added sugar. #5. Whole wheat toast Whole wheat toast is nutrient-dense with lots of fiber, protein, vitamins, and minerals. Because of these properties, it makes you feel full for a longer time and boosts your metabolism. Plus, whole wheat bread contains fewer calories than white bread, and when you toast it, the glucose level in the bread alters, which significantly lowers the glycemic index, making it more healthy for you. It is easy to incorporate into your diet as it goes with many things, easy to prepare, and is perfect for people who are always on the go. Pair it with eggs or nut butter; this will enhance the taste and nutritional value of the toast. We've made it halfway through our list, and we sincerely hope you've enjoyed it so far. Please don't forget to subscribe to my channel. Hit the Like button and ring the notification bell if you want to see more content like this. Let’s move forward with the video! #6. Nuts & nut butter A fist full of nuts is a great way to start your day as they are rich in protein, essential fatty acids, fiber, vitamins, and minerals. A study published in the journal Current Atherosclerosis Report shows that nut consumption is linked with a lower risk of cardiovascular diseases. They are also rich in antioxidants which fight inflammation and avoid cell and tissue damage. But the reason that makes them a perfect morning food is their high fiber and protein content, which gives you an abundant amount of energy to perform your daily task, helps you ward off fatigue, and reduces your daily caloric intake. Nut butter is also a good choice for breakfast. It pairs well with almost everything from fruits to smoothies to toast, and the list goes on. #7. Protein shakes If you want to have a protein-enriched breakfast but do not have enough time for preparing fancy foods, protein shakes are the way to go. They provide you with your morning dose of protein that keeps you full for a longer time, and the best thing is it is low in calories, so it is great if you are on your journey to weight loss. You can also add your favorite fruits, vegetables, seeds, or nuts to protein shakes. It is a simple and easy way to add vitamins and minerals to your daily diet. To get maximum benefits, keep an eye on the ingredients and choose the one without added sugar or artificial sweeteners. #8. Bananas We all know bananas are the superstar of potassium; However, their goodness is not limited to just that. Packed with filling fiber and other valuable nutrients, tossing some diced bananas in your fruit bowl or morning smoothie will lift your mood, giving you an energized start to the day. What makes bananas awesome is the resistant starch that supports digestive health and does not leave you feeling bloated or lethargic. As they are rich in potassium, they also lower your blood pressure and reduce the risk of a heart attack. Add them to your oatmeal with nut butter and sprinkle some seeds over them. This delicious combination will supercharge you for your day ahead. #9. Greek yogurt The nutritious, light and super healthy Greek yogurt make perfect morning food. It is rich in protein and calcium and contains probiotics that support the healthy gut and immune system. A study published in the European Journal of Nutrition shows that yogurt consumption is associated with higher nutrient intake, better diet quality, and a favorable metabolic profile. You can combine it with fruits, nuts, and seeds to make it more filling and healthy. Choose the one without any added sugar or flavors to avoid additional calories. #10. Coffee Coffee not only helps you wake up, but it is also full of antioxidants that neutralize the free radicals and reduce inflammation and damage. According to the American Diabetes Association, drinking coffee reduces the risk of developing type 2 diabetes. It also improves glucose metabolism, making it a perfect choice for a weight loss journey. But what you are putting in your coffee is also important. Keep it simple and reduce or avoid adding sugar or heavy cream. You can use non-fat or plant-based milk and enjoy a cup of healthy and delicious coffee with your breakfast. A good breakfast boosts your energy and gets you ready for the day. So never miss it and add these foods to your morning routine to notice more productive changes in your day. They are easy to prepare, healthy, and most importantly, delicious. After eating a nutritious breakfast, go for morning meditation or exercise. It will make your morning routine more healthy and positive. I know it is hard to adopt, but it will pay off forever and make your life beautiful. Will you start eating these foods after watching this video? Let us know in the comment section below. Make sure to subscribe; so you don't miss out on my future videos. Don’t forget to like and share. Stay Healthy.

- How to Eat High Protein without Wasting your Money.: Kind: captions Language: en Right now, it feels like everyone is trying to sell you protein. If you walk into a grocery store, it's slapped on every label. Scroll your Instagram feed and you'll probably see a lot of cottage cheese or the latest high protein ice cream brand. And Drew Gooden just made a great video about this where he basically bought every protein product he could find. And he raised some good points like first, protein products are generally pretty expensive, and two, most of them don't taste great. But here's the thing. Eating a high protein diet does have a lot of benefits. And personally, it has completely changed my life. It's part of what has helped me go from 240 lbs in high school to 175 lbs now. And several years ago, I pushed much lower just to see how far I could go. But I didn't do any of this by throwing back protein shakes, taking supplements, or trying out the latest protein snacks. I'll have an occasional protein bar, but 95% of my weekly protein intake just comes from normal food that I cook at home, which I find is way cheaper and much more enjoyable way to get my protein. And I'm able to do this by following three simple strategies that all build on each other. Now, before we dive into the strategies, I have three questions that you need to answer because the way that I use these strategies will probably work slightly different than how you will use them depending on the answers. And first up is why do you want to eat higher protein in the first place? Two is how much protein do you actually need? And the third question that most people miss is how much protein do you currently eat? Because eating some set of protein per day is not magically going to solve all your problems. You need some clarity on why you want to do this and how to approach it. And I'm going to give you my answers to these three questions to just guide the discussion, but they're for example purposes only. None of this is going to stick if you don't figure it out for yourself. So, let's hop in. But first, thank you to today's sponsor, Graza, where if you smell these three olive oils side by side, they all have quite different aromomas and because of that, different ways that you should use them in the kitchen. And here's why. First up is their extravirgin olive oil that can be used for cooking known as sizzle. And this olive oil is made from 100% pqual olives, but they're picked at a slightly later maturity. So there's kind of a little bit of a mellower olive aroma, but it has a higher smoke point, which is great for cooking. And this is very different from their second option, drizzle, which is a finishing olive oil that you probably wouldn't want to waste cooking with. This extravirgin olive oil is made from younger hoji blanca olives. So it kind of has this more intense almost spicy aroma that would be great for finishing on salads or pasta dishes and things like that. Then the last olive oil that has been missing from Graza's lineup is frizzle. And this is a neutral high heat olive oil that can be used for searing, deep frying, or baking. And you could use this in place of whatever your typical neutral oil is like canola, vegetable, or avocado. This is still made with 100% olives, but it's completely neutral smelling, especially compared to these other two. So, if you were like making French fries or maybe chocolate chip cookies, this is the one that you would want to grab. I mean, maybe you could try olive oil flavored chocolate chip cookies. It could be interesting, but probably not in most cases. Anyway, if you want to check out the trio for yourself, you can head to my link in the description or use the QR code on screen to get 10% off your order. And thanks again, Graza, for sponsoring this video. But now let's ask the question, why do you want to eat higher protein in the first place? Protein is not some magic macronutrient. I like to think of it as a tool that can make your goals easier to achieve. And all humans need some basic amount of protein to survive and support overall body function. Your hair, skin, enzymes, hormones, and immune system are all built on amino acids, which are the building blocks of protein. Now the minimal daily recommended is.36 g per pound. So for 175 lb person this would be roughly 63 gram or 252 calories worth of protein per day. Now beyond that eating a higher protein diet can have added benefits like allowing you to build excess muscle, stabilizing energy levels and just can make overall diet adherence and weight loss easier since gram for gram protein is more satiating than carbohydrates or fats. The thing is though, protein only helps you achieve these in the context of calorie balance, your training, and what the rest of your lifestyle looks like. So, this is where you need to ask yourself, what changes are you looking for? And for me, the answer to this question has changed throughout the years. When I first started in high school, I simply wanted to lose weight. And the real trigger for fat loss is simply eating fewer calories. But by eating higher protein, it helped me feel fuller on fewer calories. so I didn't feel starved while dieting. Then several years later, after I had already lost a bunch of weight, I wanted to see how lean I could get. So eating a higher protein diet became a shield to protect the muscle I'd already built while I stayed in that caloric deficit. Now, where I'm at today is much more just about general health. I'm not chasing bodybuilding strength goals or a specific body fat percentage. I lift a few times a week, nothing crazy. So, I want enough protein to hold on to some lead muscle and support my overall health. things like better recovery, stable energy, and just keeping my body functioning well. And this brings us to question number two. How much protein do you actually need to support these kinds of goals? And again, the answer to this question for me has changed over the years. Still to this day, the most commonly talked about number when it comes to how much protein you need per day to build an optimal amount of muscle is 1 g per pound of body weight. So 175 lbs means 175 gram or 700 calories worth of protein. But here's why this amount of protein can be problematic for a lot of people. First up, the most evidence-based number for optimal muscle growth is around 73 g per pound or 1.6 g per kilogram of body weight. And anything beyond that, there is either no benefit or extremely diminishing returns. And I will leave links to these articles and studies below. But additionally, the average person starting out and just wanting to lose some weight or get some muscle in the weight room can get great results at even 0.55 grams per day, assuming the other key factors like your total calories, your resistance training, recovery, and sleep are all dialed in. Now, there's nothing wrong health-wise eating one gram per pound of body weight or even much higher, assuming you're meeting all of your macronutrient and total calorie needs. But the reason why this amount can be problematic for many people and send them down the expensive rabbit hole of consuming protein shakes, snacks, and bars is that it severely restricts the types of food you can eat on a day-to-day basis. And I learned this the hard way a few years ago. Because you have to keep in mind, the three primary macronutrients that make up the calories in our food all work in a pushpull relationship. 1 gram of fat is 9 calories. 1 gram of carbs is 4 calories. And 1 g of protein is four calories. So if you try to increase your protein intake while keeping your calories the same, you have to either reduce the carbs, the fat, or a little of both. And what a lot of people do is try to lower our total calories per day and increase the amount of protein at the same time. And this is the exact trap that I fell into a few years ago when I wanted to see how lean I could get. I started at 165 and was like, "Well, I've got to do the 1 g per pound rule." So, 165 g of protein per day. And after like 3 days of trying out that diet, I was like, "Dude, this sucks. I cannot do it." So, I just was like, I'm going to shoot for 120 g a day and see what happens. And guess what? The difference was massive from a lifestyle perspective. We are talking about 45 g of protein difference per day or 180 calories worth of food that I can now spend on carbohydrates or fat instead of shoveling more protein. And we're going to get into a lot more examples shortly, but here's one simple one. At 1 g per pound, if I wanted a burger, I feel like I'd almost have to eat it wrapped in lettuce or something and then still add a side of plain chicken breast just to hit my protein goal. However, at 7 grams per pound, I can now have that same burger just on a bio bun, which is about 160 or so calories, and I'm still well within my targets. And for me, this made it so much easier to stick to and made the next 5 weeks fly by. And the key takeaway here is that 7 g per pound is going to be the sweet spot for optimal muscle gains. Or if you want a rounder number, maybe just say 3/4 of your body weight. However, even something like 0.55 g per pound is going to be great for the average person who trains, eats well, has a job and hobbies outside of just being in the gym every day for a few hours. Now, if you want to be a bodybuilder, elite athlete, or strength competitor, like, sure, eat as much protein as you want. But for most people, you don't really need to be as obsessed with protein as it kind of feels like everyone is today. In my personal example, it made a massive difference just lowering that target. And I was able to achieve outcomes that I wouldn't have been able to otherwise. And this leads us to the third question. How much protein are you actually eating right now? And this is the question that I didn't ask myself back then, but I later realized I was already eating around 120 or 30 g of protein per day while at maintenance calories. So, when I shifted into a caloric deficit, all I really needed to do was slightly adjust my carbs and fat, not suddenly try and skyrocket my protein, which makes a drastic lifestyle change. So, before you start blindly following someone else's high protein diet that they gave you that works for them, I would highly recommend going on a little self-exloration for yourself. Just grab a simple food logger and maybe track for 3 to 5 days. See how much protein you kind of average out per day. And then once you have the answer to that question, it makes it so much easier to implement strategy one, which is creating slightly higher protein versions of foods that you already like. When people start eating a high protein diet for the first time, a lot of them will generally swing towards two extremes. first is going to be completely cutting out the foods they actually enjoy, thinking, "Well, I can't have pizza, pasta, burgers, or anything with a high calorie count that primarily comes from carbohydrates or fat. So, they'll just avoid these foods completely and find themselves thinking back on the good old days." Or the other extreme is they'll make versions of the foods that they enjoy, but they are extremely high protein and low calorie versions. So suddenly the burger bun becomes lettuce, pizza dough is swapped for cauliflower or ground chicken, cottage cheese gets added to everything, or you start buying chalky protein desserts. And sure, both of these options are going to help you eat a higher protein diet, but personally, I don't find these enjoyable. I want my meals to still feel like real meals. I want to be able to eat burritos, fried rice, or dishes that I just may get in the mood for. I want my pizza to have bread. I want my burger to have a bun. And this is why one of the best skills that I've learned over the years is how to make slightly higher protein versions of foods that I already like. And I've shown this on the channel so many times. For example, I have this dialedin 500 calorie double smash burger made with 93 ground beef and a normal bun and cheese. I've made Chipotle style burritos where instead of loading it with a bunch of rice, I increase the amount of seasoned chicken. There's still plenty of cheese, salsas, and sour cream. I've made butter chicken where I still use some butter, but I'm not drowning it in butter. And I've made a higher protein, roughly 600 calorie pizza that is still made with those. And in all of these cases, I'm making ingredient tweaks to the carbohydrates and fat amounts that in turn bump up the protein content by anywhere from 10 to 25 g. And if you do that twice a day or a few times a week, you can easily start to get 20 to 50 g more per day. And to me, this is just a way more enjoyable way to go about life rather than trying to get rid of foods I already like or just making ultra ultra high protein versions that don't really taste much like the original. And I'll leave a link to a whole collection of recipes that I've used throughout the years if you want to look for some inspiration. And strategy 1 is such a great starting point for eating higher protein. But here's the problem. If this is the only way that you approach eating a higher protein diet, you're likely going to run into three challenges. First, your grocery bill can still stay fairly high. Sure, it's going to be cheaper than buying a bunch of protein bars and protein ice creams, but you're still buying specialty ingredients for each individual recipe. And because of this challenge two, you end up with a lot of random leftover ingredients. It could be a bag of onions, a few burger buns, a chunk of cheese, or a sauce you bought for one recipe but never use again. And lastly, you become recipe reliant. So even though you have these leftovers, it's hard to figure out what to do with them. So to combat this, we are going to use strategy one alongside strategy 2, which is flexible protein prep. And with flexible protein prep, we're no longer making contained recipes. We are prepping protein options that can be used for a variety of different meals. I've broken this down in a few different videos of mine, such as how to cook healthy meals and never run out of ideas, how to meal prep if you hate meal prepping, and how to turn chicken breast into infinite meals. And there are a couple of variations here and there, but the core idea is always the same. One, start with a protein. Could be chicken breast, ground beef, chuck roast, tofu, fish, whatever you like. Two, season that protein in a variety of ways. You could keep the base neutral, add some different spices, marinades, spice blends, whatever you want. Three, apply that seasoned protein to different form factors. So the same protein can go into a salad, a sandwich, a rice bowl, pasta, tacos, literally anything. And lastly, finish it with condiments and toppings. Sauces, herbs, crunchy things, all give each meal a totally different vibe without any extra effort. So instead of thinking in purely recipes, you're starting to think more into modular meal building blocks. And this leads to a lot of benefits. For example, you'll amortize your time and effort. So, you only do the hard and generally most time-consuming part once, which is going to be cooking the protein. But this time and effort carries you through multiple meals. And the big difference between what I'll call formal meal prep, where you kind of just make the same meal for the whole week, is that this method forces you to be creative and increase your food variety. Since you're starting with some kind of protein base, it leaves you room to naturally experiment with the leftover ingredients you may have from using strategy one, like that half of onion, jar of sauce, or a few burger buns. And instead of just listing off a bunch of examples, I'll recommend that you go check out the video where I didn't cut anything. I basically just showed how I start with two chicken breasts, but then turn them into drastically different meals. And again, strategy one and two are great on their own, but they're definitely still more geared to maybe helping you eat high protein for a day or maybe a couple of days at most. But the way to make all of these strategies sustainable over the long term is to pair them with strategy three, the high protein grocery loop. And here's why. Grocery shopping is kind of the invisible force that shapes how we eat. I mean, if you don't have food in the house, you're either stuck making a last minute run to the store for more cottage, cheese, protein, snacks, or you're just going to order some takeout. And if every grocery trip feels like you're starting from scratch with a completely new list, it's going to feel overwhelming, random, and inconsistent. And the grocery loop aims to break that cycle. It's how you build momentum over time. And the key to understand here is that your grocery shopping is not going to look perfect in week one, but over month one, month two, and over several years, this system will build and compound, and your grocery shopping will get easier over time. And here's how it works. So, step one when you start to put your grocery list together is to anchor it with general high protein staples. So, this could be one or two proteins you like, a couple of versatile produce options, a couple of dairy, pantry or spices or sauces. And these are the ingredients that are going to help facilitate flexible protein prep. Then, step two is to find one or two specific slightly higher protein recipes you can make this week. And these are from strategy one. Maybe it's the high protein smash burger and some brazed beef tacos. And you'll likely find that you already have some of those ingredients listed from step one. then you just need to fill in the gaps. So once you have the list together, you run to the store, cook the specific meals, do your flexible protein prep, and then you'll use up some of those leftover staples and recipe ingredients. And here's where the loop part comes in, because whatever is left from that initial run that you got can become the starting point for next week's list and meals. Maybe now you have some onions left, there's a bottle of olive oil that's almost used up, or there's a sauce you didn't finish in the fridge. And these leftover pieces naturally are going to inspire your shopping list and next meal idea. So you're never starting from zero. Each grocery trip gets easier, more consistent, and more tailored to how you actually eat. And over time, your kitchen naturally kind of fills up with the right kinds of food, the stuff that makes eating high protein simple and automatic. And I don't think of this as like a one-time high protein grocery haul that is forcing me to stick to this very rigid meal plan. It's an ongoing loop, but ultimately it's an imperfect one. For example, maybe I had chicken breast written down, but thighs were on sale, or I have a bunch of cilantro left from last week, so I decide to make a corn salsa this week. Or maybe some weeks I just find a new hot sauce or sauce to try. And the idea is you're creating a system while slowly building habits that support higher protein eating in the long term. And I've been thinking about doing a dedicated video around this idea of the grocery loop because I've started to realize that grocery shopping effectively really is a skill, but it's one that none of us really talk about or teach how to do it because it looks so different for so many different people. The tricky part with filming that type of video, though, is I need to kind of film everything I'm doing, not just for one week, but really multiple weeks in a row so I can show how that loop builds over time. I want to figure out a way to do I just need to find like a full month where I can kind of block off and dedicate towards that type of project. Now, we'll save that idea and video for another day. But for now, to round out this video, remember if you use all of these three strategies together and keep those three key questions I asked earlier in mind, you should be able to learn how to eat high protein not just for a couple of days or a couple of months, but in a way that is less expensive, more enjoyable, and most importantly, sustainable for the next 5, 10, 20, or 30 years. But anyway, there will be a bunch of links down below that you can use for inspiration. And thank you again to Graza for sponsoring this episode. But that will wrap it up for me in this one. I will catch you all in the next one.

- Are You Eating Enough Protein for Your Age & Goals? Women’s Optimal Protein Intake Explained: Kind: captions Language: en Let's bring protein into the conversation because in the past decades we've heard a lot about carbohydrate, low carb, high carb, fat, low fat, highfat, but protein's kind of been out of the conversation until recently. There's a lot of confusion right now about what is optimal for protein. We're hearing people push on the upwards of 3 1/2 g per kilogram, which is about 1.8 g per pound. We also hear people say no 50 gram of protein total per day for women is adequate. It's actually somewhere in between because if we look at the recommended daily allowance right now we see it's around8 kilo g of body weight. And that I want people to understand is the bare minimum that you need to consume to prevent malnutrition. When we're looking at an active individual as well as an active aging individual, we need more. When we bring our sex differences into effect, we see that women age differently than men in the fact that we have perry and postmenopause. Men age in a linear fashion. Women have a really specific point in their lives where all of a sudden their bodies responding differently. Why I'm bringing that up is because as we get older, our bodies, both men and women, become more resistant to the growth effects of protein and growth effects of exercise. So we need to look at dosing more of both. So for women as we get into our 40s onward, we are looking more at that 1 g to 1.2 g of protein per pound of body weight, which sits around that 2 to 2.2 g per kilo. When we're looking at women who are in their reproductive years, 18 to about 35 to 40, 1.6 six gram per kilogram or hitting about that.9 g per pound is the bare minimum that you want to have if you're an active individual. Of course, in times where you're trying to build through strength training, you're going to increase your protein. So, when we're looking at protein and protein effects, it's not just about building muscle and maintaining lean mass. There is a critical component of the amino acids of protein that go to help with bone and bone density. We also see amino acids are important for neurotransmitters, for nerve conduction, for so many other functions within the body. So, if we're low in protein, then we're going to have systemic effects that we might not notice right away, but down the line, there's definitely an impact, especially when it comes to brain, bone, and muscle health. I think two of the biggest things to take away from protein and the intake capacity is we've neglected it for so long and it's really difficult for so many women to wrap their heads around increasing protein and protein needs but the longer term effect of maintaining just that RDA is the face that we have of osteopenia cognitive decline cycopenia so if we start earlier by increasing our total protein intake and managing that intake across the day. We're going to have a stronger skeletal system, a stronger muscularkeeletal system, and a better brain health. If we start looking at increasing our protein intake from that8 g per pound, trying to eat our way up to at least 1 g per pound, but ideally 1.2 to 1.3 g per pound. Because lifestyle and life phase can affect how much protein you need from puberty, pregnancy, reproductive years, pmenopause, post-menopause. It's hard to condense it all into one short YouTube segment. I will refer you to the position stand we wrote with the International Society of Sport Nutrition last year on the female athlete that actually encompasses all the lifespan and how much protein you need across the day as well as pre and post exercise. So many people think that getting that one gram per pound of body weight in protein is really difficult. But if we pay attention, it's really not that difficult. We can look at how do we get 30 to 40 g at each meal, maybe 15 to 20 at each snack, and what the quality of that protein is. So if you're feeling overwhelmed with increasing protein, make small steps over the next 3 weeks. Increase your protein from what you're having now to trying to hit that 1 g per pound. is very achievable, but it does take some steps to get used to thinking about protein and putting it on your plate. So, if you have any questions, shoot them in the comments below. Thanks for watching.
