- What Happens To Your Body on High Protein Diet: Kind: captions Language: en Are you ready to instantly lose&nbsp; weight, gain muscle, and live longer?&nbsp;&nbsp; Well, no matter what anyone says, there is no&nbsp; miracle diet that can do these things for you.&nbsp;&nbsp; Staying healthy is a balance between eating&nbsp; the right foods and being active. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; a high protein diet may be a good place to&nbsp; start. There are a lot of positive things&nbsp;&nbsp; that eating protein can do for your body. But&nbsp; too much protein can cause some nasty side&nbsp;&nbsp; effects. How much is too much? And does it matter&nbsp; where your protein comes from? Let’s find out. Proteins are arguably the most&nbsp; important molecules in your body.&nbsp;&nbsp; They make up your cells, allow your body to carry&nbsp; out essential functions, and without protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’d die. There are over 80,000&nbsp; different proteins in the human body,&nbsp;&nbsp; and each one has a function. In fact, proteins&nbsp; make up around 20% of your entire body. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; it should come as no surprise that consuming&nbsp; enough protein is vital to your survival. Proteins are made up of smaller molecules called&nbsp; amino acids that are joined together and aligned&nbsp;&nbsp; in unique ways to create different shapes.&nbsp; The crazy thing is out of the 21 amino acids&nbsp;&nbsp; used by the human body to make proteins; there&nbsp; are 9 that can’t be synthesized by your cells.&nbsp;&nbsp; This means the only way to get them is by eating&nbsp; foods that have these amino acids stored in them. Which foods contain the amino acids and proteins&nbsp; I need to stay alive, you may be wondering.&nbsp;&nbsp; Don’t worry; The Infographics Show&nbsp; has got you covered. We will tell you&nbsp;&nbsp; exactly where to find the protein your body&nbsp; so desperately craves later on in the video. Let’s start with the basics. When you eat&nbsp; something, your body tears it apart, breaks&nbsp;&nbsp; it down, and then circulates the molecules and&nbsp; nutrients contained within the food around your&nbsp;&nbsp; body so that your cells can get what they need.&nbsp; For example, if you eat a slice of pork belly,&nbsp;&nbsp; the fats, proteins, and other molecules&nbsp; that are contained within the piece of meat&nbsp;&nbsp; don’t go straight to your belly. Instead, the meat&nbsp; is broken down in the stomach and intestines, and&nbsp;&nbsp; the individual nutrients get circulated to every&nbsp; part of your body via your circulatory system. Therefore, we eat to provide our bodies&nbsp; with the molecules necessary to function&nbsp;&nbsp; and keep us alive. And proteins are one&nbsp; of the most important molecules of all.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, how much protein should you actually be&nbsp; eating? And what does it do to your body? Everyone’s body is different, which means that&nbsp; everyone's daily intake of protein will need to&nbsp;&nbsp; be different to provide the optimum effect.&nbsp; Researchers suggest that the average male&nbsp;&nbsp; should consume around 56 grams of protein per&nbsp; day while females should have approximately 46&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of protein each day. However, since&nbsp; everyone is a different size and weight,&nbsp;&nbsp; there may be a better way to calculate&nbsp; how much protein you should be eating. You should be consuming around .365 grams of&nbsp; protein a day for every pound you weigh. An easier&nbsp;&nbsp; way to think about this is for every 10 pounds of&nbsp; body weight; you should eat 3.65 grams of protein.&nbsp;&nbsp; This means if you weigh 150 pounds, you should&nbsp; eat around 55 grams of protein throughout your&nbsp;&nbsp; day. However, this is just a baseline. People who&nbsp; are more active will need to consume more protein&nbsp;&nbsp; to maintain or build their muscle mass. But these&nbsp; calculations are a good baseline to start with. Surprisingly, there is still a decent amount of&nbsp; debate within the scientific and medical community&nbsp;&nbsp; on how much protein someone should&nbsp; actually consume in a 24 hour period.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some experts put the number twice&nbsp; as high as previously mentioned,&nbsp;&nbsp; but more research still needs to&nbsp; be done to justify these numbers. Regardless of what the exact amount of&nbsp; protein a person should consume is, there&nbsp;&nbsp; is an even more important question. What does&nbsp; the protein you eat physically do to your body? Since proteins play a vital role in many life&nbsp; functions and literally make up the cells&nbsp;&nbsp; in your body, a better question might be: what&nbsp; don’t proteins do? There are definitely a number&nbsp;&nbsp; of noticeable effects that a high-protein diet&nbsp; can have on your overall health and physiology. Scientists have found that high-protein&nbsp; diets end up reducing your appetite.&nbsp;&nbsp; A number of studies revealed that out of all the&nbsp; nutrients you consume on a daily basis, protein is&nbsp;&nbsp; the one that makes you feel full the fastest.&nbsp; The reason for this is that protein reduces&nbsp;&nbsp; the amount of a hormone called ghrelin in your&nbsp; body. Ghrelin is one of the molecules that tells&nbsp;&nbsp; your brain that you’re hungry. Therefore, the less&nbsp; ghrelin in your system, the less hungry you feel. Protein also boosts the levels of peptide&nbsp; YY, which signals the body that it’s full.&nbsp;&nbsp; With the reduction of ghrelin and the increase of&nbsp; peptide YY, the body feels nutritionally satisfied&nbsp;&nbsp; much sooner than you would with a low-protein&nbsp; meal. This reduces the urge to overeat,&nbsp;&nbsp; which in turn can help you&nbsp; lose weight and stay healthier. In a study conducted at the University&nbsp; of Washington School of Medicine,&nbsp;&nbsp; people who increased the number of calories&nbsp; they consumed from a protein source from 15%&nbsp;&nbsp; to 30% ate around 441 fewer calories per day than&nbsp; someone on a lower-protein diet. The craziest part&nbsp;&nbsp; was that the people in the study were&nbsp; allowed to eat as much as they wanted.&nbsp;&nbsp; The group that increased their protein intake&nbsp; just felt fuller quicker, so they ate less.&nbsp;&nbsp; What this implies is that subbing out foods that&nbsp; are higher in fats, sugars, and carbohydrates&nbsp;&nbsp; for meat or high protein veggies might make&nbsp; you feel more satisfied with fewer calories. Along with suppressing appetite, high protein&nbsp; diets can help you lose weight in another way. High protein intake has been&nbsp; shown to increase metabolism,&nbsp;&nbsp; at least for a short period of time. Even as you&nbsp; eat, your body is using energy to break down the&nbsp;&nbsp; food and circulate the nutrients to your vital&nbsp; organs and muscles. The energy expended and the&nbsp;&nbsp; increase in metabolic rate to aid in digestion&nbsp; is called the thermic effect of food or TEF. The interesting thing is that protein has a much&nbsp; higher thermic effect than most other types of&nbsp;&nbsp; molecules like carbohydrates or fats. Studies&nbsp; have shown that by eating high protein foods,&nbsp;&nbsp; your thermic effect can increase by 5 to&nbsp; 15 percent more than consuming food with&nbsp;&nbsp; little or no protein. What this means&nbsp; is that your body has to work harder&nbsp;&nbsp; to break down protein than other molecules. This&nbsp; causes you to use more energy during digestion&nbsp;&nbsp; while simultaneously increasing your metabolism. All of these factors combined can cause your&nbsp; body to burn 80 to 100 more calories a day&nbsp;&nbsp; just from eating. And this might be a&nbsp; conservative number. One study showed&nbsp;&nbsp; that a group of people eating a high protein&nbsp; diet burned on average 260 calories more per day&nbsp;&nbsp; than a group of people on a low protein diet.&nbsp; This is the equivalent of the amount of energy&nbsp;&nbsp; expended from a moderate workout session. Who&nbsp; knew that eating could be such a good exercise? And speaking of working out, protein is&nbsp; literally the building block for all your&nbsp;&nbsp; muscles. So, does eating more protein&nbsp; correlate to increased muscle size? Not exactly. However, increasing the amount of&nbsp; protein in your diet will impact your muscles&nbsp;&nbsp; in a number of different ways. Eating more&nbsp; protein does not necessarily build more muscle,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it does help maintain muscle mass and&nbsp; promote growth. If all you did was eat protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; your muscles would not magically become&nbsp; bigger. However, if you engaged in a&nbsp;&nbsp; weightlifting regiment on a regular basis&nbsp; while also increasing protein intake,&nbsp;&nbsp; your muscles would likely get larger more&nbsp; quickly than if you stuck to your typical diet. Someone who is active and trying&nbsp; to bulk up their muscle mass should&nbsp;&nbsp; definitely increase the amount of&nbsp; protein they put into their bodies.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is also another important factor to consider&nbsp; when trying to build, or more importantly,&nbsp;&nbsp; maintain muscle mass. If for some reason you&nbsp; need to stop lifting weights or reduce your&nbsp;&nbsp; time working out due to an injury or life just&nbsp; getting in the way, you should still maintain a&nbsp;&nbsp; high protein diet. The rationale for this is that&nbsp; if you go from being highly active to less active,&nbsp;&nbsp; you will begin to lose weight as your muscles&nbsp; shrink. However, a high protein diet can slow&nbsp;&nbsp; the rate of and even prevent muscle loss&nbsp; if the right types of food are consumed. Perhaps the biggest selling point for&nbsp; high-protein diets is that not only&nbsp;&nbsp; will they help you lose weight, but they&nbsp; will help you keep the pounds off as well. Over the course of a year, high-protein diets&nbsp; seem to be much more effective at helping people&nbsp;&nbsp; maintain weight loss. Now, it is important&nbsp; to remember that just eating a lot of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; will not cause you to lose weight instantly.&nbsp; However, there may be some huge benefits if you&nbsp;&nbsp; stay active, eat the right types of protein,&nbsp; and keep track of your calories. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; a study that tracked 130 people on high-protein&nbsp; diets over the course of a year found that they&nbsp;&nbsp; lost 53% more body fat compared to a control&nbsp; group of people on a regular-protein diet. In a clinical trial conducted by the Department&nbsp; of Human Biology at Maastricht University in the&nbsp;&nbsp; Netherlands, researchers found that high-protein&nbsp; diets reduced weight regain by around 50%.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is huge for people who want to lose weight&nbsp; and keep the weight off. As many dieters know,&nbsp;&nbsp; losing the weight is only half the battle;&nbsp; it’s keeping the weight off that can be hard.&nbsp;&nbsp; But it seems from a number of studies that&nbsp; a high-protein diet and healthy lifestyle&nbsp;&nbsp; may be one of the best ways to do just that. You probably have heard that one side&nbsp; effect of a high protein diet is that&nbsp;&nbsp; it can be damaging to your bones.&nbsp; But is there any truth to this? This myth probably originated from the idea&nbsp; that protein can increase the acidity level of&nbsp;&nbsp; the body slightly, which in theory could cause&nbsp; calcium to leach from your bones. To be fair,&nbsp;&nbsp; if your blood’s acidity was high enough,&nbsp; it most certainly could cause the calcium&nbsp;&nbsp; on your bones to break down and weaken&nbsp; your skeletal system. But if your blood&nbsp;&nbsp; ever ended up at that high level of acidity,&nbsp; you’d be dead, so it really wouldn’t matter. What long-term studies have shown is that&nbsp; both animal and plant protein can actually&nbsp;&nbsp; help people maintain bone mass as they&nbsp; get older. According to some research,&nbsp;&nbsp; people who maintain a high protein diet over&nbsp; extended periods of time also seem to be at a&nbsp;&nbsp; lower risk of osteoporosis and bone fractures.&nbsp; It is important to point out that this does&nbsp;&nbsp; not mean you should immediately start consuming&nbsp; massive quantities of beef to increase your bone&nbsp;&nbsp; health. There are better sources of protein,&nbsp; which we will break down for you in a bit. The same thing goes for this&nbsp; next health benefit. It is all&nbsp;&nbsp; contingent on what type of protein you are eating. It may seem counterintuitive, but a high-protein&nbsp; diet can actually lower your blood pressure.&nbsp;&nbsp; The exact mechanism for this is&nbsp; not completely understood yet,&nbsp;&nbsp; but in one study, it was found that a&nbsp; high-protein diet did not just lower blood&nbsp;&nbsp; pressure but also reduced LDL cholesterol and&nbsp; triglycerides. This is important because these&nbsp;&nbsp; factors in high levels can be a recipe for&nbsp; heart attacks, strokes, and kidney disease. Although a high-protein diet can&nbsp; have harmful effects on the kidneys,&nbsp;&nbsp; you probably don’t need to worry too&nbsp; much as long as you are in good health. Doctors recommend that people with kidney&nbsp; diseases reduce the amount of protein they eat.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because high amounts of protein&nbsp; can exacerbate problems in kidneys that&nbsp;&nbsp; are already damaged. However, the same&nbsp; thing cannot be said about people with&nbsp;&nbsp; healthy kidneys. It is always&nbsp; important to follow the advice&nbsp;&nbsp; of doctors and medical professionals&nbsp; when they recommend lifestyle changes.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, no studies have connected high protein&nbsp; consumption to causing damage to healthy kidneys. Protein may even be able to reverse&nbsp; and repair the damage done to the body. Since it is the building block of&nbsp; your cells, mussels, and organs,&nbsp;&nbsp; eating more protein actually provides your body&nbsp; with the resources it needs to help repair itself.&nbsp;&nbsp; Several studies have even claimed that after you&nbsp; suffer an injury, consuming more protein could&nbsp;&nbsp; help speed up the time it takes to recover. Some&nbsp; researchers even claim that if you are on a high&nbsp;&nbsp; protein diet before you get injured, it may&nbsp; cause your body to heal faster than someone&nbsp;&nbsp; who is not. More analysis needs to be done, but&nbsp; it is possible that in the future, doctors will&nbsp;&nbsp; recommend foods high in protein after an injury,&nbsp; along with other already established treatments. So, we know that high protein diets have several&nbsp; immediate and short-term benefits for the body.&nbsp;&nbsp; But what about as you get older? It may&nbsp; sound crazy, but a high protein diet in&nbsp;&nbsp; the middle stages of life can have lasting&nbsp; benefits even into your more mature years. It is no secret that as you age, your body&nbsp; becomes weaker. Your muscles start to shrink,&nbsp;&nbsp; and your bones become more frail. However,&nbsp; studies have shown by eating more protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; these effects can be mitigated. It is important&nbsp; that you also maintain some form of activity as&nbsp;&nbsp; well because if you don’t use your muscles,&nbsp; they will begin to atrophy no matter how&nbsp;&nbsp; much protein you eat. But eating a high-protein&nbsp; diet during your life and into your later years&nbsp;&nbsp; may help your body retain some of its strength&nbsp; and keep you in better overall health. There are definitely a lot of positives to eating&nbsp; a high protein diet, but is there a downside?&nbsp;&nbsp; Unfortunately, yes, so before&nbsp; you go on a meat buying spree,&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure to watch the rest of this video. As we said before, there is no miracle diet that&nbsp; can solve all of your weight and health problems.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is especially true of high-protein diets that&nbsp; require you to cut out all other macronutrients&nbsp;&nbsp; from your meals. You should steer clear of any&nbsp; diet that tells you to remove all carbs or fats&nbsp;&nbsp; from your life. In these types of situations,&nbsp; you could develop nutritional deficiencies&nbsp;&nbsp; such as low fiber, which can cause&nbsp; headaches and constipation. Therefore,&nbsp;&nbsp; a high-protein diet does not mean you should&nbsp; remove all other types of food from your life. As we said before, the type of protein you’re&nbsp; eating matters. And there are definitely some&nbsp;&nbsp; foods that are better than others.&nbsp; You should not be eating all red meat&nbsp;&nbsp; or full-fat dairy foods because they can&nbsp; cause an increased risk of heart disease.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because these types of food are often&nbsp; full of saturated fats and high in cholesterol. So, this brings up the question: what&nbsp; type of foods should you be eating in&nbsp;&nbsp; your high protein diet? You obviously want&nbsp; to receive the benefits of consuming more&nbsp;&nbsp; protein but don't want to put yourself at&nbsp; risk for high cholesterol or heart disease.&nbsp;&nbsp; Luckily, The Infographics&nbsp; Show has got you covered. The best meats to eat to get your&nbsp; protein fix are a mixture of birds,&nbsp;&nbsp; fish, and one other animal that you won’t believe. Chicken is a relatively low-priced meat that is&nbsp; healthy and can provide enough protein to meet&nbsp;&nbsp; your daily requirements. Chicken contains around&nbsp; 31 grams of protein in every 100 grams of meat.&nbsp;&nbsp; The best thing about chicken is&nbsp; that about 90% of the calories&nbsp;&nbsp; in the meat come directly from protein.&nbsp; We discussed earlier how important this&nbsp;&nbsp; is for speeding up your metabolism&nbsp; and reducing daily caloric intake. However, an even healthier option for high protein&nbsp; meat is fish. Yellowfin tuna contains around 30&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of protein per 100 grams of meat. Other&nbsp; fish possess slightly less protein per serving,&nbsp;&nbsp; but not by much. However, the benefit of&nbsp; eating fish as part of a high protein diet&nbsp;&nbsp; is that they also contain omega-3 fatty acids and&nbsp; vitamins such as D and B2. Omega-3 has shown to be&nbsp;&nbsp; extremely good for your heart and reduce plaque&nbsp; buildup around blood vessels. This means that&nbsp;&nbsp; fish is a two-for-one meal that provides both high&nbsp; amounts of protein and essential vitamins as well. Another seafood that is high in protein&nbsp; is shrimp. These crustaceans contain&nbsp;&nbsp; 23 grams of protein for every 100 grams of meat.&nbsp; They also might provide a cheaper way to get&nbsp;&nbsp; your daily protein from seafood as shrimp tend&nbsp; to cost less per pound than higher-end fishes. The most surprising animal that can&nbsp; serve as a good source of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; isn’t a fish, bird, or mammal.&nbsp; Instead, it is an insect. Crickets are an excellent source of protein. They&nbsp; contain around 60 grams of protein for every 100&nbsp;&nbsp; grams of crickets consumed. Right now, crickets&nbsp; tend not to be the main source of protein for any&nbsp;&nbsp; diet. However, as scientists and nutritionists&nbsp; work to make crickets more mainstream,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is changing. The great thing about&nbsp; these insects is that they are plentiful,&nbsp;&nbsp; reproduce rapidly, and have minimal impact on&nbsp; the environment. When they are eaten whole,&nbsp;&nbsp; crickets don’t really have much of a taste other&nbsp; than whatever seasoning is put on them. Some&nbsp;&nbsp; companies are even using crickets in their protein&nbsp; bars and powder. Regardless of your feelings on&nbsp;&nbsp; bugs, crickets may be the most sustainable,&nbsp; cost-effective source of protein we have. Meat is not the only way to get your protein,&nbsp; though. If you are a vegetarian or just looking&nbsp;&nbsp; for plant-based options, there are plenty of&nbsp; high-protein plants that will allow you to&nbsp;&nbsp; meet your daily intake goals. These foods&nbsp; also are healthier than eating most meats&nbsp;&nbsp; and can either be used as a substitute or&nbsp; an extra source of protein during a meal. One of the richest plant proteins is seitan.&nbsp; This durable food is made from the gluten of&nbsp;&nbsp; wheat and contains around 25 grams of&nbsp; protein for every 100 grams of seitan.&nbsp;&nbsp; It also boasts the highest amount of protein per&nbsp; gram out of any plant-based alternatives to meat. Tofu is also high in protein as well.&nbsp; Tofu is actually made of coagulated soy&nbsp;&nbsp; milk that has been pressed together into a block.&nbsp;&nbsp; Although this doesn’t sound super appetizing,&nbsp; Tofu has the ability to fit into almost any dish&nbsp;&nbsp; as it has very little taste of its own and its&nbsp; texture can change based on how it’s cooked.&nbsp;&nbsp; The best part about tofu is that it not only&nbsp; contains high amounts of protein but it is&nbsp;&nbsp; rich in iron and calcium as well. In 100 grams&nbsp; of tofu, there are around 20 grams of protein. Beans are also a great protein source for&nbsp; anyone trying to stay away from meat. Most beans&nbsp;&nbsp; constrain around 15 grams of protein per cup. And&nbsp; since beans can be cooked in a variety of ways,&nbsp;&nbsp; they can easily be added to pretty much&nbsp; any meal to boost your protein intake. Medical professionals recommend that most of&nbsp; your protein should come from plant sources.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is because they tend to be a healthier option&nbsp; than meat due to the fact that plants contain many&nbsp;&nbsp; nutrients and vitamins your body needs. If&nbsp; you only eat meat, you will definitely have&nbsp;&nbsp; a high-protein diet, but that will be about it.&nbsp; There is no substitute for a well-balanced diet,&nbsp;&nbsp; so make sure to incorporate different protein&nbsp; sources in your meals throughout the day. This brings us to a very important&nbsp; question. How much protein is too much?&nbsp;&nbsp; And what side effects can&nbsp; consuming too much protein have? It is always possible to have too much of a&nbsp; good thing in life, and protein is no different.&nbsp;&nbsp; When you consume too much protein the&nbsp; risks outweigh the benefits. For example,&nbsp;&nbsp; although protein has been shown to have&nbsp; no long-term effects on healthy kidneys,&nbsp;&nbsp; it can still cause other problems&nbsp; in this part of the body. Eating&nbsp;&nbsp; too much protein can lead to kidney stones,&nbsp; which are incredibly painful until removed. Depending on the size of the stones, a doctor&nbsp; may need to use a laser to break them up into&nbsp;&nbsp; smaller pieces. There are two main ways&nbsp; that the kidney stones can then be removed;&nbsp;&nbsp; both are painful enough that the patient&nbsp; needs to be given anesthesia. One method is&nbsp;&nbsp; known as a cystoscopy, where the doctor inserts a&nbsp; cystoscope into the urethra to locate and remove&nbsp;&nbsp; the stones. You can imagine that having anything&nbsp; stuck up your urethra would be unpleasant. Another method of removing kidney stones&nbsp; is called percutaneous nephrolithotomy.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is when the doctor cuts a hole in your side&nbsp; and kidney and inserts a tool called a nephroscope&nbsp;&nbsp; into it . This device then allows the doctor&nbsp; to look around and remove any stones they find. As mentioned before, eating too much red meat can&nbsp; also lead to high cholesterol and heart problems.&nbsp;&nbsp; Therefore, it is important not to restrict&nbsp; your high protein diet to just one type&nbsp;&nbsp; of meat or protein source. There is still&nbsp; disagreement amongst medical professionals&nbsp;&nbsp; as to how much protein is too much, but there&nbsp; are definitely some guidelines to follow. For the average person, it is recommended&nbsp; you not eat more than 0.9 grams of protein&nbsp;&nbsp; for every pound you weigh. This means that&nbsp; a 150-pound person should not consume more&nbsp;&nbsp; than 135 grams of protein in a day. And this&nbsp; is the very upper limit. That being said,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you are someone who is extremely active&nbsp; or an athlete, you can probably get away&nbsp;&nbsp; with eating slightly more protein as your&nbsp; body will use it to repair and build muscle.&nbsp;&nbsp; It is when the body becomes oversaturated&nbsp; with protein that problems start to arise. There are some key steps you should take&nbsp; before starting a high-protein diet,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially if you are going to try and&nbsp; consume the upper limits for your body weight.&nbsp;&nbsp; The first is to discuss the decision&nbsp; with your doctor. They will likely want&nbsp;&nbsp; to run some tests just to make sure your&nbsp; kidneys are healthy and in working order. You will also need to do your research.&nbsp; We have tried to provide you with some&nbsp;&nbsp; of the best food options to eat while on a high&nbsp; protein diet, but there are many other choices.&nbsp;&nbsp; Depending on where you live and what type of&nbsp; foods you like, there are likely other sources of&nbsp;&nbsp; protein that might be better for you. You can also&nbsp; talk to a nutritionist who may be able to give you&nbsp;&nbsp; further guidance. It is important to remember&nbsp; that although some meats have higher amounts of&nbsp;&nbsp; protein than other foods, fish, nuts, and beans&nbsp; can all be healthy supplements to any meal. Regardless of what high-protein foods&nbsp; you decide to eat, you should still&nbsp;&nbsp; include fruits and vegetables in your diet.&nbsp; They may not have as much protein as meat,&nbsp;&nbsp; but they are rich in vitamins&nbsp; and minerals your body needs. You also should identify exactly why you are&nbsp; starting your high protein diet. If your goal&nbsp;&nbsp; is to lose weight, you don’t need to consume as&nbsp; much protein as someone who is working to bulk&nbsp;&nbsp; up and gain muscle. Or maybe you are just planning&nbsp; for the future and want to have a healthier body&nbsp;&nbsp; in the long run. There are many benefits to eating&nbsp; a high protein diet, but there can also be risks.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why it never hurts to discuss your&nbsp; dietary plan with medical professionals. Now watch “What Happens To Your Body&nbsp; When You Don't Eat (Fast).” Or check&nbsp;&nbsp; out “What Happens To Your Body If You Never Move.”

- Eating Protein | Why Eating Enough Protein Helps Busy Women Lose Weight: Kind: captions Language: en In my last video, I talked about carbs and fats and why there are good ones and bad ones in both camps. In today’s video, I’m going to talk about the third macronutrient which is protein, and I’m sure we all know that protein is good for us, and that we definitely need it in our diets. But why is that? Today, I’m going share with you the 6 main benefits of having protein in our diets. I’m Andrea Robertson, I have 4 university degrees, including Nutrition, and I’m here to inspire amazing women, just like you, towards heathier and happier lives. I’ve helped hundreds of my patients navigate the complexity that is nutrition, to help them fully understand the best nutritional approaches for their body to help them to lose weight, gain energy, and feel great in themselves again, so now it’s your turn. If you watch this video all the way to the end, you will learn the main reasons why protein is so important. Plus you’ll learn different ways to get protein into your diet to help you lose weight and stay fit and for longer. But before we get started, for the best online barre, yoga, adult ballet and barre inspired HIIT workouts, as well as lots of nutritional and wellness information, subscribe to my channel and hit the bell to be notified when I post a new video every single week. Ok, lets get into it. Benefit No 1. Research has shown that diets high in protein have been found to reduce appetite, reduce hunger and reduce snacking compared to low protein diets. Because of this, eating protein also promotes weight loss. If you fill 30% of your plate with protein along with healthy carbs and fats, it will automatically be easier for you to lose weight. Ok, let’s get a little scientific…we have a hormone released in our stomach called ghrelin. It is the hunger hormone, so when our body releases ghrelin we want to eat, and we want to eat NOW! Eating protein actually reduces the amount of ghrelin released. Proteins also increase the release of satiety hormones such as gastric inhibitory polypeptide and peptide YY, the hormones that help make you feel full. Good right!?!? So protein reduces the hormones that make you feel hungry, and increases the hormones that make you feel full. Benefit No 2. Protein boosts your metabolism and helps with fat burning. Eating all foods actually boosts your metabolism for a little while, however protein does it for longer. This happens through a process called thermogenesis and protein is great at increasing this, thereby burning more calories for nothing just by increasing protein in your diet. Benefit No 3. Proteins reduce cravings. Cravings are different to normal hunger. I don’t know if you are anything like me, but sometimes I sit at home after dinner, and I literally can not think of eating anything other than chocolate. It’s usually happens when I’m tired and I haven’t been taking the time to prepare well balanced meals. Cravings are hard to beat, they really are, so protein helps reduce them from starting in the first place. Benefit No 4. Protein, along with carbs and fats, helps provide our body with energy by being broken down to glucose. In my last video, I talked about how our brains, along with red blood cells and the nervous system, need glucose as their sole source of energy, so if your body is low in carbohydrates, protein will provide this much needed energy source to these important body parts. If I get a little scientific again, which you know I love doing, it’s through a process called gluconeogenesis…which literally means the making of new glucose. Benefit No 5 The proteins we eat provide amino acids, or building blocks, to our body, for our body to then create it’s own proteins. Some of these proteins in our body act as enzymes. Some enzymes can break down substances, but others also build substances, such as bone. Evidence shows that people who have a diet higher in protein tend to maintain bone mass as they age, therefore reducing the risk for osteoporosis and associated fractures. My family has a really high genetic tendency towards osteoporosis, so along with regular weight bearing exercise, I’m always on to having protein in my diet, even if just for this benefit. And No 6 The last benefit that I want to share with you today is one that most people know about, and its how protein helps build muscle mass and strength, as well helping with body repair, and keeping you fit as you age. As I just mentioned, the proteins we eat in foods provides our body with amino acids, the building blocks for our muscles and body tissues. Our body can make 11 of the amino acid, but 9 of the amino acids, called essential amino acids, can only be gained through eating protein from foods. So now that we have gone over the 6 main benefits for eating protein, I want to talk about different sources of dietary protein and how you can then add more protein into your diet. Animal sources of protein include meat, fish, poultry, eggs, milk, and yoghurt. Think roast chicken, BBQ steak, omelettes, goats cheese on salad, or tuna with anything. No eating of puppy dogs though, they are just good for scarves. Vegetarian , or plant based sources, of protein include vegetables, seeds, nuts, grains and legumes. Think pasta made purely from beans, chia puddings, nuts sprinkled onto salads, tofu stir-fry, miso soup and hummus with veggie sticks. But be careful to not crowd out other foods by eating too much meat and eggs. Fruits and vegetables are Soooo important, and provide our body with so many other essential nutrients. Ok, now you understand the benefits of protein, and how to get more protein into your diet, I want you to also remember that exercise is a very important component of living a healthier life for longer. If you need some extra help on clean eating recipes and exercise tips, I’ve created a simple and easy to follow program called Total Body Tone Up in Under 3 Weeks. estrogen it has been well documented that as the ovaries make less less estrogen fat gets redistributed to your abdominal area so when a postmenopausal woman gains weight that weight gain is most likely belly fat and it's not really clear why this happens but as far as overall weight gain after menopause we do see that when estrogen is suppressed a woman experiences a slowdown of her resting metabolic rate which of course can be a contributing factor and we also know that while the ovaries are the biggest producers of estrogen the fat cells are a secondary source so after menopause your ovaries no longer produce enough estrogen so it is possible that the body looks at the addition of fat as a way to regain some of its loss estrogen so outside of hormone replacement therapy it is a reality that after the age of 50 women have a natural tendency to put on belly fat however this belly fat and weight gain do not have to be a reality if we control another important hormone called insulin regardless of your age insulin is the hormone that has the most influence over whether your body will store fat and that fact is why I often refer to insulin as your fat storing hormone so when you eat a meal it is insulins job to move the nutrients that you consumed into your cells where they can either be burned immediately as energy or stored for later use in places like your muscles or fat cells when we were young and active our cells were very sensitive to insulin so every time insulin would drop off nutrients our cells would gladly receive them and use them but as we age ourselves become less sensitive to insulin so when insulin brings the nutrients to the cell the cell resists insulins attempt to drop it off and that is referred to as insulin resistance insulin resistance is a very common problem has two important consequences cravings and weight gain cravings for carbohydrates increased because the cells are not taking in the energy they need so your body determines that there is a need for more energy to be eaten carbs are the quickest form of food energy so they are what to you crave right the cookies cakes and candies breads pastas those types of foods weight gain increases with insulin resistance because you end up with too much insulin in your system and this happens because your cells are not responding to insulin so your pancreas pumps out more of it and in an attempt to kind of force the cells to open and that chronically high insulin encourages fat storage and insulin resistance is not just an age-related issue it is made worse by increased belly fat which is a result of lower estrogen as well as other factors like poor sleep which can be a consequence of menopause and of course other lifestyle factors are going to factor in like a long history eating a high refined carbs I at lack of exercise and chronic stress which produces more cortisol and contributes to belly fat and so what we see is that menopause has the potential to create the perfect storm of weight gain so to make weight loss easier after menopause we need to disrupt the cycle and we do that by targeting the things we can control now we're not going to turn back time and naturally rejuvenate our estrogen output but we can work with our bodies to allow them to use estrogen more efficiently and lessen the impact of insulin resistance and that starts with a diet that is high in healthy fats all of your cells are surrounded by a fatty cell membrane so making sure that your diet includes healthy fats and excludes unhealthy fats is important for the health of your cell membranes and therefore the healthy cell membranes allow for the transport of hormones into your cells which is where they do their work and I can mention as well that the addition of healthy fats will also help with hunger satiety so cravings are decreased now I have a video on healthy and unhealthy fats but essentially the whole fats we get from nuts seeds avocados fatty fish and high quality meats provide beneficial fats and the fats to stay away from in my opinion are vegetable oils like soybean oil that is so common in processed and fried foods nowadays to more effective ways you can overcome weight loss resistance after menopause are to utilize a low carb diet and intermittent fasting and these strategies allow you to work around insulin resistance because they naturally decrease your body's need for insulin carbohydrates particularly sugar and refined carbs like muffins and sandwich breads and pastas caused a spike in insulin which puts your body in that fat storing mode that we talked about earlier so avoiding sugar and refined car allows your insulin levels to remain steady which gives your body a chance to release fat intermittent fasting is a simple strategy that involves shortening the number of hours you eat during the day I have a video on intermittent fasting and women over 50 that you might want to watch for some more information but the basic premise of intermittent fasting is simply eating all of your calories for the day in a shorter eating window so instead of grazing from early morning to late evening you might not start eating until 11 a.m. and then stop by 7:00 p.m. so the basics of how to include following a high fat low carb diet and practicing intermittent fasting for at least a few days out of the week and when you have those Basics in place you start to see a snowball effect happening in the opposite direction so your body is more apt to burn fat rather than to store it for instance we know from dr. Sachin pandas work on circadian rhythm that limiting late-night eating which naturally comes when you practice intermittent fasting is an effective way to help with sleep quality because your body is able to complete digestion before you go to bed better sleep quality brings more energy which makes you more active and more able to cope with stress and these things all work together and the changes can be seen quite quickly so while I acknowledge that menopause throws us some curveballs there are ways to make weight loss easier if you're looking for a guide my coaching program has low carb high fat menus worked out for you so there is no guesswork and it shows you how to incorporate intermittent fasting for better results and of course you can also join in the discussion with members of the program that are your age and share your journey so if you are interested in the program I will leave links here for you thanks for watching please subscribe to my You Tube channel and I will be back soon with another video to help you reach your goal thanks

insulin sensitivity leading to easier weight gain with the drop of estrogen we also see a relative increase in androgen levels in this hormonal imbalance leads to more intense hunger signals and cravings for comfort food those hormonal factors are coupled with a time in life when physical activity and muscle mass are on the decline causing a reduced need for calories so eating the same way we of normal health and it has also been found to be an effective way to reduce belly fat here's how you can start benefiting from intermittent fasting right away note the time that you finish eating dinner tonight and let that time mark the end of your eating for the day when you wake up tomorrow morning postpone breakfast until 14 hours have passed for instance if you finish eating dinner at 7 p.m this evening put off breakfast until 9 a.m tomorrow morning now there's many different fasting schedules that you can follow this schedule is referred to as 14 10 fasting because you are fasting for 14 hours and restricting your calorie intake to the remaining 10 hours of the day and i chose this method for you because a 14 hour fast is manageable and also because this was the fasting routine shown to reduce belly fat in the study that i referred to earlier like the study participants i encourage you to restrict your eating window to 10 hours a day for 12 weeks for full results the second change that you can make to start losing menopause belly fat is to reduce your carbohydrate intake if you remember from the menopause perfect storm scenario one of the weight loss barriers created by lower estrogen levels is reduced insulin sensitivity when your cells are less sensitive insulin levels increase in your blood since insulin is a fat storing hormone when it is high you are storing energy much of which ends up as body fat however the opposite is also true when insulin is low you create an internal environment that favors fat release therefore the way to get around insulin resistance is to lessen insulin's workload you do that by choosing foods that do not spike insulin levels carbohydrates especially refined carbs cause the most significant spike in insulin so they are the ones to limit here's how you can start using low carb eating to your advantage tonight for dinner swap high carb choices like pasta pizza and bread for a meat or fish entree and non-starchy vegetables such as broccoli cauliflower or asparagus this low-carb evening meal will keep insulin levels low you'll then follow that meal with your 14-hour fast because there's no food coming in during those fasting hours insulin remains low keeping your favorable fat burning state going strong even as you sleep when you get comfortable with your low carb dinner you can work on reducing your overall carb intake for the day if you are new to low carb eating start by aiming for no more than 100 grams of carbs per day the third thing that you can do today in fact right now to get your - I’ve Changed My Diet (Menopause Made Me Do It): Kind: captions Language: en-US For the past eight years, I have been&nbsp; following a low-carb diet and practicing&nbsp;&nbsp; intermittent fasting. I still do those things.&nbsp; But, blame it on menopause or just Father Time,&nbsp;&nbsp; now that I am in my later 50s, I have had&nbsp; to make some modifications to feel my best.&nbsp;&nbsp; This video details the specifics&nbsp; of how my diet has changed and why. [Intro Music] With age comes wisdom, but age&nbsp; also brings some physical changes,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I have noticed the changes&nbsp; in my body that come with age. To be specific, belly fat is&nbsp; a real thing after menopause;&nbsp;&nbsp; eating for health becomes more real with age,&nbsp; and body composition shifts toward fat rather&nbsp;&nbsp; than muscle if you are not watching. Those&nbsp; are the main reasons that I changed my diet. To keep pace with my changing body,&nbsp; I have adjusted my daily and monthly&nbsp;&nbsp; macronutrient ratio, which I will explain.&nbsp; I have also taken steps to support muscle,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I have shifted my snack choices. What has not changed is my focus&nbsp; on the 3Es and eating whole foods. Eating and physical activity should always&nbsp; be looked at as a long-range adjustment,&nbsp;&nbsp; not a quick fix. Long-range adjustments are&nbsp; achievable for anyone when the plan you have&nbsp;&nbsp; meets the 3Es, meaning your plan is easy for&nbsp; you to follow, enjoyable, and effective. When&nbsp;&nbsp; you have those three things in place, there is&nbsp; no need or desire to go back to your old habits. The changes I have made fit my 3Es, and&nbsp; adding GEM to my daily routine has as well. GEM is a real-food multivitamin that provides&nbsp; nutrients your body can readily absorb and use. These GEM bites have become an&nbsp; enjoyable part of my daily routine. It looks like a treat, and it is,&nbsp;&nbsp; but it is a treat that is packed with more&nbsp; than 20 whole food vitamins, minerals,&nbsp;&nbsp; superfoods, prebiotics and probiotics to&nbsp; help fill the gaps in your daily nutrition. On my diet, I eat both plant and animal&nbsp; foods. A flaw is that I am routine,&nbsp;&nbsp; so I tend to eat the same salad and vegetable&nbsp; side dishes each day. GEM gives me a variety&nbsp;&nbsp; of nutrients that I do not get from my typical&nbsp; diet, and I love that about these GEM bites. You can eat a bite any time of day, I&nbsp; enjoy one after lunch. It has a nice tangy,&nbsp;&nbsp; citrus flavor and a really&nbsp; satisfying chewy texture. Right now, is a great time to try these real-food,&nbsp;&nbsp; nutrient-dense bites because GEM&nbsp; is offering a special deal off your&nbsp;&nbsp; first month! Go to dailyGEM.com/DRBECKY&nbsp; and use my code DRBECKY at checkout! The first change I have made involves shifting&nbsp; my macros. I have done this daily to include&nbsp;&nbsp; more protein and monthly by incorporating&nbsp; three-day periods of intermittent carnivore. As we age, one of the biggest threats to quality&nbsp; of life is the loss of muscle and muscle strength.&nbsp;&nbsp; Eating more protein after you reach the age&nbsp; of 50 supports muscle mass preservation,&nbsp;&nbsp; and when paired with resistance&nbsp; exercises that tax your muscles,&nbsp;&nbsp; it preserves muscle strength&nbsp; and encourages growth. You have likely heard that more&nbsp; protein is good as we age. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; what does that look like? It is more&nbsp; simple than you may imagine. For instance,&nbsp;&nbsp; when I started eating low-carb, I&nbsp; followed a 60-20-20 macronutrient ratio,&nbsp;&nbsp; meaning 60 percent of my daily calories came&nbsp; from fat and 20 percent came from both protein&nbsp;&nbsp; and carbohydrates. My diet today is closer to&nbsp; 60-25-15. So, my fat intake has stayed the same,&nbsp;&nbsp; while my protein intake increased by 5 percent&nbsp; and my carb intake decreased by the same amount. A 5 percent increase in daily protein will depend&nbsp; on your calorie intake, but for most, this means&nbsp;&nbsp; adding 15 to 25 grams of protein. For example,&nbsp; I have added a protein shake after I exercise,&nbsp;&nbsp; which boosts my protein intake by 13 grams, and&nbsp; I have added a hard-boiled egg or a couple of&nbsp;&nbsp; ounces of chicken or meat to my daily salad&nbsp; for an additional 6 to 10 grams of protein. I have also incorporated three-day periods of&nbsp; intermittent carnivore into my monthly diet. The Intermittent Carnivore Diet,&nbsp; as I call it, is a diet regimen&nbsp;&nbsp; that introduces brief periods of eating zero&nbsp; carbs or as close to that goal as possible. On a carnivore diet, you eat meat and&nbsp; other animal products. There are no&nbsp;&nbsp; plant-based foods because all&nbsp; plants contain carbohydrates. I love this dieting strategy and utilize it&nbsp; twice a month. I have a video describing what&nbsp;&nbsp; I eat and how I do it. I will leave a link&nbsp; for you if you would like all of the details. To give you an overview for this video,&nbsp; the concept of intermittent carnivore&nbsp;&nbsp; is simple. For three days, you eat only&nbsp; carnivore foods. So, food choices include&nbsp;&nbsp; meats (i.e., beef, bison, fish, lamb, organ meats,&nbsp; pork, poultry, seafood), eggs, bone broth, animal&nbsp;&nbsp; fats (i.e., butter, lard, tallow), and some dairy&nbsp; products (i.e., full-fat milk, cream, cheese). I do not count calories on my carnivore days.&nbsp; One of the nice things you find about the diet&nbsp;&nbsp; is that your food intake is self-limiting.&nbsp; There are not a lot of choices, and the&nbsp;&nbsp; choices that are allowed are high in protein&nbsp; and fat, two very hunger-satisfying nutrients. The second change I have made is&nbsp; adding creatine to my daily routine. Creatine is a supplement that supports&nbsp; muscle growth and strength. I have&nbsp;&nbsp; used it periodically for years, but&nbsp; now I use it as a daily supplement. Creatine came into significance in the&nbsp; body-building world because it helps&nbsp;&nbsp; your muscles produce energy. But creatine&nbsp; supplementation has been shown to act as a&nbsp;&nbsp; possible countermeasure to the menopausal-related&nbsp; decrease in muscle, bone, and strength. And while some research suggests that&nbsp; the supplement alone can improve muscle&nbsp;&nbsp; mass and strength in older adults, the results&nbsp; are best when paired with resistance training. One thing I will note about creatine is that&nbsp; it can increase water retention in muscles.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is a good thing, not a bad one. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; that water retention may show up as a&nbsp; couple of pounds of weight gain when&nbsp;&nbsp; you step on the scale. It does not cause you&nbsp; to gain fat. Quite the opposite, but you may&nbsp;&nbsp; gain weight from the water retention in your&nbsp; muscles initially and muscle growth over time. The third thing that I have changed is my snack&nbsp; choices. I found that I felt better and had an&nbsp;&nbsp; easier time maintaining my weight when I replaced&nbsp; my typical low-carb snacks with protein snacks. When I started eating low-carb, typical snacks&nbsp; included full-fat yogurt with blueberries,&nbsp;&nbsp; raw or toasted nuts, and dark chocolate. Today,&nbsp;&nbsp; I feel my best when I satisfy between-meal-hunger&nbsp; with a beef stick, hard-boiled eggs, or cheese. These trade-offs have helped me achieve&nbsp; my daily macronutrient shift toward more&nbsp;&nbsp; protein and fewer carbs without sacrificing&nbsp; the 3E's. Grabbing a beef stick, boiled egg,&nbsp;&nbsp; or a few slices of cheese is&nbsp; easy, enjoyable, and effective. I like routine as much as anyone, but we&nbsp; live in dynamic bodies that adapt as time&nbsp;&nbsp; passes. If your diet feels stale, you&nbsp; are not progressing toward your goal,&nbsp;&nbsp; or the things that have worked in the past are no&nbsp; longer working, be open to making some changes. Over the past year, I have added more daily&nbsp; protein, incorporated monthly intermittent&nbsp;&nbsp; carnivore days, added creatine, and shifted&nbsp; to protein snacks. These changes have been&nbsp;&nbsp; effective for me, and I hope they give you some&nbsp; ideas that you can incorporate into your life. Are there things that you have&nbsp; shifted in your diet over time? If so,&nbsp;&nbsp; please share them in the comments below.&nbsp; Thanks for watching and thanks to GEM for&nbsp;&nbsp; sponsoring this video. I will leave a&nbsp; link to the GEM bites and the videos&nbsp;&nbsp; I mentioned in the description or more&nbsp; area below. Have a great rest of your day.

We can't rely on our hormones for that anymore. We have to look for an external stress. So this is where exercise comes in. So we need a higher dose to invoke muscle protein synthesis and bone regeneration, nerve regeneration. We're looking at a therapy to attenuate change. If we're looking at hormone replacement, that could be thyroid, that could be uh premature ovarian failure, that we need to have some um estrogen, progesterone. So the body is not going to have vasom motor symptoms and is not going to have mood changes and is not going to really have an incredible amount of body composition change. Do my joints stop hurting? Do I stop having all these sleep interruptions? Do I stop having to worry about my bones? And if you're putting in the right lifestyle changes to maintain bone health, yes. On the other side, everything becomes a new normal without the pain and dysfunction because it's the shift in hormones that's creating so many different issues with every system of the body. So, one of the big things that dieters encounter are progress plateaus. And so, we can define a progress plateau as four to possibly eight weeks of no change in body composition, despite good compliance to the program. With that definition out of the way, intervening and overcoming or managing a plateau is really sort of this individualized process that needs to be looked at case by case, where, for example, if somebody feels like they're in the midst of a plateau, and they have been on program, then there's really only two reasons the plateau happened. So, reason number one is that their compliance was inconsistent. So, poor compliance is number one. Or number two, they have reached energy equilibrium. So, they've reached a genuine and bonafide new maintenance point. But, there's something that is overarching with the plateau concept that people need to understand. we automatically look at progress plateaus as something negative. When people need to their perception of what a plateau is. And the plateau is just the body doing its job. When we look at the body as an adaptive survival unit, then homeostasis is a big part of that. So, if the body achieves homeostasis, then hallelujah, we're we're going to live. We're going to survive. So, if for example somebody has a lot of weight to lose, let's say somewhere to the order of over 20 lbs, 20, 40, 60 lbs to lose. They have to understand that multiple plateaus will be encountered en route to their ultimate goal. the way that the body changes is always going to be this surge, slow, stop pattern. And it just continues this way. And with every progressive plateau, the surge part gets shorter. And the slowing part and then the plateau part gets longer. So, you can think of it as staircases and landings. So, with each successive plateau, the staircase gets shorter, the landing gets longer. But, it's supposed to go like that. And the plateaus should be getting longer because the ultimate goal after all is a plateau of sorts. And when people come to realize that, then they can look at plateaus as what I call maintenance practice. So, if they have that mental shift from seeing plateaus as this negative thing where I need to go sniff out the next great diet or the next great product, they can look at it as, all right, so the plateau is a good thing, the body is doing its job. Now, we have an opportunity to practice And think about it this way, too, Stephen. Anybody can get weight off. But, weight loss maintenance really is the issue. So, the better you get at weight loss maintenance, then the more you can win the game. How do I get good at weight loss maintenance? All right, so in order to properly maintain, you have to properly get there. And so, properly getting there means that you have to do your best to maintain your lean body mass while you're losing body fat. So, what happens to a lot of people when they diet is they lose a lot of lean body mass along Lean body mass, you mean muscle? >> mass. >> Muscle mass. Along with their fat mass. muscle tissue is something very important to keep on the body while you're losing fat. Because muscle is we can look at it as our metabolically active Every tissue is metabolically active, even even body fat. But, muscle tissue specifically is the center of our dietary fuel usage. It is the metabolic engine of the body, if you will. And so, if you're losing muscle tissue, you're really losing metabolic leverage while you're losing body fat. So, the way that I infer that is that muscle's very greedy. Yeah, it it takes a lot takes up a lot of calories. So, if I lose my muscle, when I get when I drop my weight, it's almost like the greedy guy who who sucks up all my calories is no longer there. And so, I'm quite likely just to to rebound quite quickly. That's a great analogy. And and it's accurate, too. And in fact, there is a phenomenon that in the literature, it's called collateral fattening. And that happens when the body senses an energy crisis at the end of a diet where you've lost a bunch of muscle mass. The body senses that, oh my gosh, we just lost a bunch of precious tissue. We have to do whatever we can to get it back. And so, your hunger signals ramp up. And your body kind of behaviorally and even metabolically does what is necessary for you to feed that back as soon as possible. And this does not necessarily happen, certainly not to that kind of magnitude, if you keep your muscle mass while you're losing body fat. You don't experience this collateral fattening type of phenomenon where people just rebound like crazy because their appetite is out of control at the end of the diet. So, the way that you preserve muscle mass during the fat loss process is a couple things. So, you have to make sure that your rate of weight loss isn't indicative of something that's too quick. about a half a percent to a full percent of your body weight lost per week is as fast as you really want to go. So, what roughly a pound a week. Uh some people who start off heavier, okay, 2 lbs a week is fine at the very beginning. But, you generally don't want to lose more than 1% of your total body weight per week because then that increases the chances that you're losing an undue amount of muscle mass along with your fat mass. And so, if you can in essence control the weight loss rate, then you will keep your lean mass. Now, the other two things that need to be going on at the same time are you need to be resistance training and you need to be consuming enough protein. So, strength training and Enough protein, you got resistance training, and then you have sort of a top speed limiter on how much weight that you lose per week. And 1% loss a week 1% loss a week is spectacular, actually. For most people. Even a pound a week, even half a pound a week, you're looking at like in 2 years you lost 50 lbs. Most people took like two decades to put on that 50 lbs. >> So, you actually don't want to lose weight too quickly or else you are susceptible to rebound. What is your perspective on the ketogenic diet? What is it good for? What is it not good for? Is it good at all? It is a very effective way to lose weight and And that's for a few big reasons. First of all, if somebody goes from their typical Western dietary pattern to the ketogenic diet, then they're automatically cutting out a lot of highly processed, hyperpalatable carb-fat combo junk foods and snacks that are just energy dense, easy to overconsume mindlessly. That's the good thing about the ketogenic diet, in addition to, well, they're finally eating enough protein now. And so, along with the increase in protein comes an increase in satiety and a better hunger control. Now, the negatives of the ketogenic diet, the big one is that the majority of people who engage a ketogenic diet, they don't do it permanently. For one reason or another, they're no longer on the keto diet. And this is reflected in research as well, even in vulnerable populations who would stand to benefit from from that level of restriction. So, usually what happens in research is you take you take a group two groups of subjects, and one is on the high-carb, low-fat control diet, and one of them is on a ketogenic diet, which can be achieved by a maximum of 50 g of carbohydrate in the day or less, then you're on the keto diet. So, what happens at the 12-month point in the diet, and sometimes at the 6-month point, the keto group is now consuming about two to three times more carbohydrates than the original 50-g assignment. So, they rebound? They just insidiously creep up the carb intake. There's something about the ketogenic diet that the majority of people who engage it just can't stick to it. - Visceral Fat | What Is It & How to Get Rid of It: Kind: captions Language: en visceral fat is what most of us think of as belly fat it is the term used to describe the fat that builds up within the abdomen visceral fat cannot be pinched but it does expand your waistline crowd your internal organs and actively contribute to disease in this video i explain why this type of fat is harmful and how you can get rid of it there are two main types of fat that get deposited on your body subcutaneous fat and visceral fat the word subcutaneous literally means under the skin and the term visceral refers to your viscera or internal organs now at the beginning of the video i mentioned that a common name for visceral fat is belly fat and that is true and how most of us would describe it however it is possible to have both types of fat stored in your midsection generally speaking the fat you can pinch is subcutaneous whereas visceral fat lies behind the abdominal muscles within the cavity that holds the liver stomach and intestines now the crowding of your internal organs is one of the reasons that this type of fat is undesirable but even more concerning is the fact that visceral fat is more metabolically active than subcutaneous fat in this higher level of metabolic activity increases the release of inflammatory substances and free fatty acids into your blood which increases your risk of health problems like heart disease type 2 diabetes and a related condition called insulin resistance insulin resistance is a common barrier to fat loss insulin is your energy storing hormone when you develop insulin resistance your cells no longer respond as they should to insulin and as a result your body cannot utilize carbohydrates and other food nutrients as efficiently as they once did and this leads to chronically high blood sugar and insulin levels that block the release of fat from fat cells so when we get too much visceral fat you can see that it hampers fat loss putting us into this vicious cycle where these undesirable conditions work together to perpetuate themselves fortunately a low-carb diet disrupts this cycle by keeping your blood sugar low and essentially lessening the amount of work that insulin is required to do and this works because blood sugar and insulin rise to differing degrees depending on the macronutrient breakdown of the foods that you eat of the three macronutrients that supply your body with energy carbohydrates are the ones that cause the most significant rise in blood sugar fats cause little if any rise and protein has a moderate impact therefore eating a low carb high fat moderate protein diet will prevent blood sugar and insulin spikes allowing your body to move into the low insulin state needed to release fat from your fat cells in fact many studies have shown that low-carb diets are more effective than low-fat diets for reducing visceral fat and an interesting point to note about the research is that lowering carbs was effective even when the reduction was modest for instance in this study participants consumed 43 percent of their daily calories from carbohydrates now many of you who have been interested in low carb dieting will recognize that as a fairly high percentage with a more typical range being less than 25 of your calorie intake however despite this modest reduction in carbs participants who consumed the lower carbohydrate versus the lower fat diet lost more visceral fat and 4.4 percent more total fat another way to get rid of visceral fat is by practicing intermittent fasting which is a weight loss strategy that involves splitting your day between a period of eating and fasting and the mechanism by which fasting works is similar to that seen with a low carb diet by choosing low carb foods you prevent the insulin spikes that block fat loss when you fast there's no food coming in to influence your insulin level keeping you in that state that favors fat burning in fact studies have shown that intermittent fasting is notably effective for reducing belly fat a clinical study found that limiting the number of hours you eat in a day to 10 hours was effective for treating metabolic syndrome which is a cluster of conditions that include increased belly fat and a review study published in 2016 showed that four weeks of fasting reduced total weight bmi and waist circumference preventing metabolic disorders that are common in older women and beyond food getting regular exercise provides both direct and indirect metabolic advantages that will help you reach your goal of losing abdominal fat weightlifting and other exercises performed against resistance have been shown to improve insulin sensitivity and this increased sensitivity is enhanced when resistance exercises are combined with aerobic exercises like biking swimming and using a treadmill however when directly targeting visceral aerobic exercise may have the edge over other forms of exercise when it is performed at a moderate or high intensity level carrying around excess visceral fat increases your risk of disease and leads to insulin resistance making fat loss harder but you can get control of visceral fat and get rid of it by combining fasting in a low-carb diet you attack visceral fat with two very effective tools and then as you start to feel better and gain energy you can enhance your results with exercise there is a lot to learn when it comes to putting together an effective low carb diet on my website i have two 21 day challenges that provide you with daily low carb or keto menus so you learn low carb dieting by doing it and i will share links to the challenges here in the video and below in the description area please subscribe to my channel before you go and have a great rest of your day

dynamic sitting may be upon one of the great options to help people move more while at work i think it's really cool if you can fidget more you can burn more calories this is called neat non-exercise activity thermogenesis which basically means you're moving you're burning more the final thing on our list that is really great is to use cold cold exposure is your friend if you want a better metabolism and it's really interesting as i said in the beginning we were talking about the our body's goal primarily is to maintain homeostasis and what this means balance in the system and one of the biggest things we need to maintain homeostasis with is our core temperature your body's always trying to maintain core temperature so if you've ever gone outside and it's very cold and you didn't have enough clothing one of the first things your body does you start to shiver shivering is like fidgeting your muscles are doing these micro contractions and what they're doing is they're burning fat they're burning carbohydrates to produce heat to maintain core temperature so when we take a cold shower or we maybe go on a cold plunge our body is going to get the shivering response we can burn more calories but cold is cooler than just burning calories from shivering what it also does is activates a special kind of fat that we have on our bodies called brown fat now we have white fat which might be the adipose tissue you can pinch maybe around your abdomen and this kind of fat has many functions but it primarily stores in the form of those free fatty acids brown fat is different it is brown because it's packed loaded with mitochondria these cellular powerhouses that do all this metabolism for us and produce energy and what happens is when we get cold exposure our body can actually produce more brown fat and make brown fat more active by increasing these brown fat enzymes that burn more calories this is why cold can absolutely be your friend if you're looking to lose weight and increase your metabolism a simple way to do it is to take a cold shower so maybe you do your regular shower you're on hot and at the end of your shower for one to two minutes you might go on cold it's an easy way to start or you can get to the point of taking some ice plunges and ice baths there's also great benefits to that and the final bonus i want to say that's not necessarily increasing your metabolic rate per se but is really good for your metabolic health is to start taking walks after you eat a big meal what the research shows is after we eat a meal let's say it does contain some carbohydrates our blood sugar levels start to rise and when we actually walk after a meal what happens is our body can actually take that blood sugar and take that normal spike that we experience and actually shuttle some of that sugar into our muscles into our legs as we're walking this helps stabilize your blood sugar improves digestion improves circulation and smooths out that blood sugar and insulin response so although that's not classically increasing your metabolism like caffeine per se it is improving your metabolic health which i think is a really good thing overall these are great strategies great little tidbits and i do hope that you take one of two of these things and you start applying it into your life but the bigger picture here is you need to develop a lifestyle a daily rhythm every single day where you're getting these things into your body right you're getting the right things in the morning with the good nutrition you're getting enough sleep you're having enough water you're doing things like the cold exposure you need to develop the rhythms and the habits otherwise this stuff is just information and let's face it information without action does jack for your plays a big role in repair fat burning and yes&nbsp;&nbsp; your metabolism so what does strength training&nbsp; actually look like it can be as simple as body&nbsp;&nbsp; weight exercises like squats push-ups or planks&nbsp; which is your sleep hormone keep your room cool&nbsp;&nbsp; and dark your body sleeps better at around 18&nbsp; to 20° C or 65 to 68° F do not eat too close to&nbsp;&nbsp; bedtime late night eating raises insulin and body&nbsp; temperature both of which can mess with deep sleep&nbsp;&nbsp; so try to stop eating at least 3 hours before bed&nbsp; and try a windown routine magnesium herbal teas&nbsp;&nbsp; journaling or even stretching can help to lower&nbsp; cortisol before bed and yes alcohol can make&nbsp;&nbsp; you feel sleepy but it actually wrecks your sleep&nbsp; quality and increases nighttime blood sugar spikes&nbsp;&nbsp; so if you're serious about fixing your metabolism&nbsp; scrolling bad news at night it tells your body to&nbsp;&nbsp; release cortisol cortisol is your survival hormone&nbsp; it raises blood sugar increases appetite breaks&nbsp;&nbsp; down muscle and promotes fat storage especially&nbsp; around your belly it's your body saying "Hold&nbsp;&nbsp; on to everything we might need it." than a minute take magnesium especially glycinate&nbsp;&nbsp; or citrate because these help to regulate cortisol&nbsp; improve sleep and support relaxation and set&nbsp;&nbsp; screen boundaries late night doom scrolling&nbsp; stimulates your brain and raises cortisol when&nbsp;&nbsp; it should be winding down so if you're doing&nbsp; everything right and still feeling stuck do&nbsp;&nbsp; not skip this step your metabolism doesn't just&nbsp; can start to shrink your eating window gradually&nbsp;&nbsp; and most importantly don't obsess over the clock&nbsp; focus on consistency not perfection timerestricted&nbsp;&nbsp; eating gives your body what it used to have&nbsp; naturally a break from digestion a break from&nbsp;&nbsp; insulin spikes and from constantly processing food&nbsp; and in return your metabolism gets to pause and&nbsp;&nbsp; reset all right so let's wrap this up real quick&nbsp; five is to stop eating all day long give your body&nbsp;&nbsp; a break with a 12 to 14 hour overnight fast now&nbsp; you don't need to do all of these perfectly and&nbsp;&nbsp; you don't need to do all of them all at once&nbsp; - Avoid Muscle Loss After 40 | Protein Diet, Yoga & Strength Tips | Dr Hansaji: Kind: captions Language: en Namaskar. Did you know that after the age of 30, adults lose 3 to 5% of their muscle mass every decade, and after 40, this loss speeds dramatically. The surprising part is it's not just about looking less toned. It directly affects your strength, balance, metabolism, and even how long you live. The real mistake most people make after 40 thinking that just eating less or only walking every day is enough to stay fit. The truth is that if you're not actively protecting your muscles, you are slowly losing them, and with it your energy, your vitality, and your independence. But here's the good news. With the right food, the right type of exercise, and little smart recovery, you can actually stop muscle loss and stay strong into your 50s, 60s, and beyond. The first and most important step is to give your muscles the building block that they need is the high-quality protein. At this age, your body doesn't absorb protein as efficiently as it used to, which means that you need to be more mindful of your diet. If your meals are only rice, roti, vegetables without enough protein, your muscles will keep breaking down. What you should do is include natural protein-rich food in every meal. Moong dal, paneer, tofu, curd, nuts and seeds, all these help rebuild and repair muscle tissue. Even something simple like taking a handful of roasted chana or pumpkin seed as a snack makes difference. One simple recipe you can make is moong dal chilla stuffed with paneer and spinach. Moong dal is light, easy to digest, and packed with plant protein. Paneer adds calcium and slow digestive protein, while spinach gives iron and magnesium to support muscle function. Just soak moong dal overnight, grind it into a batter, spread it like dosa, and stuff it with clean sauteed paneer and spinach. This way you're giving your muscle a complete nourishment in a form that is tasty, easy to make, and filling even on busy morning. And if you prefer something quick to drink, you can try a banana almond protein smoothie. Blend one ripe banana, 8 to 10 soaked almonds, half a cup of Greek yogurt or soy milk, and a spoon of chia seed. This gives you potassium, magnesium, healthy fats, and good protein in one glass. A perfect way to begin the day without feeling heavy, yet keeping your muscles strong. And next thing you must not ignore is doing some weight-bearing exercises or doing some asanas. Many people over 40 thinks exercise should only means walking or light yoga. Those are wonderful, but if you completely skip weight-bearing movements, your muscles won't get signals to grow and stay strong. Remember, muscles follow the principle of use it or lose it. You don't have to lift heavy weight in gym to see the result. Even simple bodyweight asanas done at home like Utkatasana, Adho Mukha Svanasana are enough to activate the major muscle group. Just 15-20 minutes three to four times in a week will make a huge difference. When you give your body this kind of resistance, the protein you eat is directed in building muscles rather than getting stored as fat. These weight-bearing practices also do something deeper. It keeps your bones strong, improve balance, and even helps regulate hormones like testosterone and growth hormone, both of which naturally decline after 40. This is why those who combine good protein intake with regular strength training look and feel much younger than their age. And then, don't forget recovery. Many people think more activity is always better, but if you don't rest well, your muscles don't repair, and you feel constant fatigue. Prioritize good sleep, stay hydrated, and include food rich in magnesium like green leafy vegetables and seeds. These help your muscles relax, prevent cramps, and make sure you wake up with energy instead of stiffness. You see, muscle loss after 40 is not inevitable. If you nourish your body with the right protein, engage your muscles with resistance practices, and give them time to recover, you can maintain your vitality, independence, and confidence into later years. So don't make a mistake of neglecting your muscles. Protect them, and they will protect you.

to Lifestyle factors we can change adding up we have to remember that nothing works forever and often what we got away with even when we were younger is now coming back to haunt us as our body and lifestyle evolves we've got to adjust how we feel and train that's why I'm going to share six tips to help you boost your metabolism and lose fat at any and every Age first focus on hydration many of us know we should drink more water but the struggle is real to actually change its habit not only is it confusing to know how much water to drink but it's hard to stop yourself during the day to get water when it isn't really a part of your routine or you're not really thirsty for it however proper hydration becomes more important as we get older and even start the hormonal changes of perimenopause especially if you're training hard you want to consider consuming more than the general recommendation of 50 of your body weight in ounces you want to even chew for 70 of your body weight in ounces of water consumed every day a one tip to help you drink more water is to set out a filled water bottle by the coffee maker to remind you to drink more water even as you make your coffee but you can improve your hydration by not only drinking more water but also by consuming more water through your foods to consume more high water content foods make a salad of cucumbers bell
