same results that's why increasing our protein intake to 30 to 40 grams per meal is especially post workout can be so key the harder you train the more you want to focus on a higher protein intake to make sure you're able to repair and recover more efficiently from your training if we aren't able to recover and rebuild properly we may see our hard work in the gym not pay off the way we'd like in terms of strikings and muscle growth which can impact our metabolic health and protein is not only key because of its muscle building benefits but also because it keeps the feeling Fuller for in a calorie deficit to lose weight this can make sticking to our nutritional plan easier long term which allows consistency and time to work their magic plus protein has a higher thermic effect than the other macros this means that your body burns more calories to digest and utilize protein for other bodily functions and to keep your tissue strong and healthy it's also important to find your food quality balance I'm a big believer in working in the foods you love I love having a rice crispy treat or Reese's Peanut Butter Cup or ice cream as dessert and I'll plan these things in first when I want them I also think we often sabotage ourselves with this clean eating pressure where we feel like we can't have things we enjoy and we eliminate Foods arbitrarily that aren't even necessarily an intolerance we personally struggle with but we do need to find a balance quality fuel helps our body function best and we always want to seek to balance enjoying life and functioning optimally we need those essential micros to not only feel our best and stay healthy but even to keep our metabolic rate higher and help us lose fat a few micronutrients you might want to focus on more if you're working to lose fat and rev that metabolism are choline magnesium vitamin D selenium and zinc choline is involved in the process of lipolysis or fat loss helping to break down fat into smaller pieces to be burned as energy eggs beef red potatoes and kidney beans are all great sources to include magnesium is involved in nearly everything but when it comes to fat loss and Metabolism it helps control insulin and glucose which both impact fat storage it can also help with water retention and bloating especially as you're increasing protein and making other dietary changes pumpkin seeds chia seeds almonds and spinach are all great sources vitamin D is also key to include but it's harder to boost through our food consumption even if we try to get out of the sun daily as we get older we want to make sure boosting our intake even with a supplement Studies have shown that low levels of vitamin D are linked to higher rates of obesity and both selenium and zinc are key for proper thyroid functioning impaired thyroid functioning will lead to a slower metabolism to increase your intake of selenium include foods like Brazil nuts yellowfin tuna and halibut and to increase zinc consider oysters pumpkin seeds pork loin and even oats while I've already talked about three key nutritional tips the best results happen when our diet and workouts work together that's why you can't ignore the importance of your training for your metabolic Health when designing your workouts stop doing body part splits design progressions that work your upper and lower body same workouts full body workouts or anterior posterior splits can be beneficial if you're looking to keep your metabolism healthy as you get older without having to spend hours in the gym six days a week not only are these workouts more efficient but you'll Target multiple large muscle groups at each session to help you more efficiently build muscle and burn more calories while I never like to focus on training is just a time to burn more calories this extra calorie burn because you're working more big muscles per session to build more muscle isn't a bad added bonus and by doing these splits you can also increase your training frequency for areas over the course of the week training a body part two to three times per week over simply doing more for an area in a single session may help you see better muscle gains that training frequency of two to three times a week can help you grow lean muscle for especially stubborn areas it can also help you train the area at a higher intensity each session performing a higher overall quality of work as too often we simply include more training volume and a workout that becomes wasted as we fatigue over the session so design workouts that allow you to Target different areas each and every session but that include work for both your upper and lower body the next training tip is to focus on low reps and heavy weights too often we turn to more cardio or metabolic workouts we want to lose fat they make us feel out of breath and like we worked hard but muscle is what Stokes a metabolic fire to build that muscle we need heavy loads and progression and movements that challenges our muscles to tear them down so they have to rebuild stronger that's why you want to work down in reps and up and weight during your training and while using a diversity of rep ranges is ideal don't ignore that maximal strength rep range of one to five reps even if muscle hypertrophy and fat loss are your goals increasing our strength and allows us to move more weight during our training sessions which will improve our muscle gains so while you might have heard that 6 to 12 reps is best for muscle hypertrophy and you do want to include this rep range working lower in reps and higher in weights will help you improve your strength and this will allow you to be stronger to lift more when you do work in that 6-12 rep range consider including a single heavy compound lift at the start of your workout for four to five rounds of three to five wraps resting three to five minutes between rounds follow that up with other compound accessory exercises in that six to twelve rep range to Target specific muscle groups you've already started fatiguing with that first lift the final tip is to Sprint more this high intensity interval work is so key to our conditioning and metabolic Health it's a great way to burn more calories in a very efficient way as well and if you perform short intervals of work of even just 10 to 20 seconds with three to five times the rest you can really train for Speed just don't get caught up in doing a bazillion rounds you don't want to feel beat down from these short sessions less is more think even just eight to ten rounds of Sprints go all out and fully recover you want to train for Speed and that means not working before you fully recover when you include Sprints in this way not only can you help keep your metabolic grade higher even increasing your calorie burn after the sessions but you'll help improve your Mind Body Connection to even get more out of your lifting this explosive power helps you more efficiently of the findings from this massive 2022 study on daily energy expenditure and over 6 000 subjects from diverse backgrounds that was published in science one of the world's most reputable journals this figure Compares body weight here on the x-axis with total energy expenditure or what Fitness people would call metabolism each one of these blue and yellow dots represents a different person blue dots term it's possible that the extra calories you burn from drinking more water could be compensated for for example you could drink more water but then subconsciously move around a little less so that the net boost cancels out at least partially I'm also not a fan of Force drinking water if you overdo it with ultra high water intakes within a short time frame you can develop water intoxication or 24 hours so I wouldn't draw any strong conclusions from it also there are at least two studies on green tea and long-term fat loss and both found no benefit so while you may get a small metabolic metabolic boost however the actual numbers are pretty underwhelming if you sit neck deep in an ice bath for 10 minutes you burn about 28 calories again for comparison you'd burn 14 calories just sitting at room temperature so you're looking at about a 14 calorie Boost from the cold bath cold baths are also going in the probably doesn't work category but what about building muscle you've likely heard that muscle burns more calories than fat and this is true three times the amount in fact at rest one pound of fat Burns about two calories per day while one pound of muscle Burns about six calories per day I did some quick math based on my current body composition and it looks like if I just sat down down all day the fat on my body would burn 24 calories and the muscle on my body would burn 480 calories that's a 456 calorie difference not bad at all let's consider you as an example let's just say you're a relatively new lifter and over the course of the next five years you put on 30 pounds of muscle if you burn 2 500 calories per day before you'd burn 2 680 calories per day now after adding the new muscle so a 180 calorie difference that's about four extra kiwis you could eat per day so far this is the biggest metabolic booster we've seen and so I'm going to put building muscle in the it works category okay so what about reverse dieting this has become very popular over the last few years reverse dieting is exactly what it sounds like the reverse of dieting instead of decreasing calories you gradually increase calories with the goal of rebuilding your metabolism now I'm planning on doing a full video on reverse dieting in the fall so for now I'll just say that I'm a bit skeptical of it for sure I have seen plenty of cases where people have increased their caloric intake by several hundred calories while maintaining their body weight but my main gripe with reverse dieting is that I just don't think it's necessary to go slowly after a diet I think it makes much more sense to go straight to your new maintenance right away then once you're at your new maintenance you can more gradually increase your caloric intake from there of course at a certain point you'll reach the upper limit of your Dynamic maintenance range and at that point you'll start gaining weight now if you're eager to learn more about reverse dieting I'll link an article by Eric Trexler down below that more or less lines up with my stance on the topic as of now I'm going to put reverse dieting in the might work category and you can subscribe and stay tuned for my full video on it okay so what about meal frequency does eating more meals throughout the day keep the metabolic furnace burning it doesn't seem to no this 2012 study had subjects spend three days in an isolated respiration chamber while eating either three meals per day or 14 meals per day with the same total calories and found no difference in energy expenditure there's also this 2015 meta-analysis pooling 15 studies which also found no significant difference in fat Mass from eating one to two three to four or five plus meals per day even though from this graph it does look like the higher meal frequency was better that result is being dragged up by a single study this one from Ewell and colleagues and the authors note that once you remove that study from the analysis the difference goes away now you can interpret that as you wish but I'm personally not convinced that a higher meal frequency gives a meaningful boost in metabolism and I'm putting you know frequency in the probably doesn't work category okay so what about cardio cardio obviously burns calories however research shows that cardio also causes something called Energy compensation this means that when you burn more calories through cardio your body subconsciously Burns less calories through meat or non-exercise activity thermogenesis this is the component of metabolism that includes activities other than exercise so stuff like fidgeting and bobbing your head to music for example let's say on a normal day you fidget and Shuffle away 500 calories through meat but today you decide to go for a 30 minute Jog and the jog also Burns 500 calories what happens next is that your body will sense the Jog and subconsciously move around a little less now you only burn 350 calories through meat of course you still get a net caloric burn from including the jog but it is offset to some degree now there are large individual differences in energy compensation but on average for every 100 calories you burn by doing cardio you'll only actually increase your daily energy expenditure by 72 calories and this is why I prefer to use diet as the main tool for establishing a caloric deficit and use cardio as a secondary tool as with cardio you do tend to get less caloric bang for your buck still even with energy compensation in mind cardio does burn calories and as such it belongs in the it works category the next thing I want to touch on is weighted vests there's some cool science behind this the general idea is if I weigh 160 pounds right now and I start wearing a 20 pound weighted vest my body starts to think that I weigh 180 pounds and burns calories like 180 pound person would recent evidence has suggested distance of a system of sensors in the body called the gravitastat which senses changes in loading on the bones if these sensors get the idea that you're now a heavier person it'll increase your caloric expenditure and decrease hunger levels my friend and natural Pro bodybuilder Eric Salazar did a case study with exercise scientist James Krieger where he wore a 34 pound weighted vest for 90 percent of his waking hours during a competition prep he got down to an incredibly shredded five percent body fat by sort of tricking his body into thinking he was 34 pounds heavier than he really was and in comparison to past diets he says he didn't have to suffer as many of the bad side effects of getting shredded so I'm tempted to put weighted vests in the it works category because I think it probably does however I'm going to leave it under the might work category for now until we get more published research lastly there are two other less sexy but still very effective metabolic strategies that I'd like to mention first avoid dieting on very low calories the more aggressively you cut the more your metabolic rate decreases so generally speaking you should aim to lose about 0.5 to 1 one percent of your body weight per week and the more you go above this Zone the more likely it is that your metabolism will start tanking slow dieting definitely works second you should try to smuggle in extra non-exercise activity where you can this is where the weighted vest might be helpful but there are other things you can do like Park further from the grocery store take the stairs over the elevator and regularly stand up and stretch at your desk I call these little activities throughout the day meat smuggling and I'd also put it in the it works category it's also worth keeping in mind that even if you implement some of these strategies and you still have a relatively slower metabolism you can of course still lose weight in fact research shows that metabolic rate isn't nearly as predictive of successful long-term weight loss as many people think people with faster metabolisms can fail to lose weight in people with slower metabolisms can succeed to lose weight and that things more reliably associated with successful long-term weight loss are being Physically Active regularly weighing yourself weight training and making Lifestyle Changes another thing that's been helping thousands of people lose fat while keeping track of their metabolism is the macro Factor app if you aren't aware I'm a part owner of macro factor and I've been using it to guide my own fat loss Journey where I've lost almost 30 pounds relying entirely on the app's algorithms macrofactor is so effective because it'll update your diet based on your unique metabolism whether that's faster or slower all you have to do is lock your weight and your nutrition and the app will give you the ideal nutrition targets for your goal and there's a growing community of people online sharing their own success stories from using the app other methods like online calculators or smart watches can be off by hundreds of calories per day and macro factor is much more reliable because it responds to your individual weight changes exactly like a coach would and then makes the appropriate adjustments based on your metabolism specifically macrofactor also has the easiest and fastest food logger on the market we've done comparison experiments to test this it has a super fast Barcode Scanner and AI features that allow you to describe meals that are more difficult to log for example if you eat out at a restaurant that doesn't have the nutritional info you can use the AI describe tool to log what you ate we also massively expanded our European food database recently which I know a lot of you guys have been waiting for so if you'd like to try out macro factor for One study showed that eating the protein first actually helped to lower blood sugar by up to 45% and insulin up to 50%. That simple switch helps your body use food for energy and not store it as fat. So, make sure and focus on clean quality protein, fats, and fibers. include them as many meals as you can and be mindful about the order that you eat them. Habit six. So, we focused on what you're eating. Now, it's time to talk about when you eat because if you're eating all day long, your body never gets a real chance to burn up that fuel and burn up fat. Most people eat from waking up to bedtime. There's coffee and there's creams and there's Starbucks runs and donuts in the morning at 6:00 a.m. and God knows for breakfast and muffins and toast. And then we have a snack at 10 o'clock and then we have lunch at noon. Then we have another snack at 3:00 and an energy drink. And then we have dinner at 6. Then we've got to do a dessert or a late night snack while we're watching TV at 9:00 or 10 o'clock at night. Here's the problem. Every time you eat, you spike insulin. And when insulin is high, fat burning mode is shut off. So what's the fix? Creating an intentional rhythm to your meals. That means spacing them out, cutting the snacks, and giving your body time to reset, lower the insulin, digest the nutrients, use the nutrients, and burn fat. That's the real reason this works. And the science backs it up. In a 12-week study, people who shortened their eating window to 10 hours a day lost over 7 pounds, dropping nearly 2 in off their waist, 3% of their total body fat. And they didn't change what they ate, just when they ate. A concept I coined called rotational eating also increases growth hormone by over 1,200% shows one study. That hormone tells your body to burn fat and protect muscle. You need growth hormone men and women. It's your body's way of saying no food, no problem. I'll start pulling from fat stores. It also activates your body's internal cleanup crew called autophagy. And this is what cleans up damaged cells, old proteins, and toxins that are oftentimes stored in fat. That's critical when it comes to fat loss because as fat cells shrink, they release toxins and junk into your bloodstream. If your body can't process that junk, your weight comes right back on. So, also note that it's not just about how long you eat, it's about how late you eat. One study found that eating the same dinner at 10 p.m. instead of 6 p.m. led to higher blood sugar and significantly less burned fat overnight. Same food, different timing. One gets stored, the other gets used. So, how do you space your meals apart? You don't have to do a fast for 20 hours. There is some benefit for the rotational eating of skipping some meals, but if you want to keep wellfed, not starving yourself, you just need to give your body regular breaks. So, here's what I would start with to feel full all the time and properly time this rhythm. Stop eating two to three hours before bed. This is the number one thing to do. Allow your body enough time to process that last meal of the day. Eat three balanced meals a day. No snacking in between. Just a breakfast, some protein, maybe a little bit of fat, maybe a little bit of fiber, solid lunch, solid dinner. Stop. And then aim for 12 to 14 hours overnight of no eating. This is rotational eating. This is a little bit of a fast from 6:00 p.m. when you had dinner and you didn't eat the next morning until 7:00. You just got yourself 13 hours of not eating. If you get a little stuck, cycle in breakfast as a liquid only, just protein coffee or fat only. Just having a little bit of coffee with a little bit of butter and coconut oil with collagen protein powder blended together. That protein coffee kind of replaces the meal and extends your food fasting till lunchtime. That'll help break a plateau. Ideally, you space your meals 4 to 6 hours apart. You got a breakfast at 8:00 a.m. You got a lunch at 12:00. You don't have dinner till 6. If you're hungry between meals, drink water. Drink lemon water. Have some coffee. Your body's thirsty, not hungry. So, this is the habit that ties everything else together. Because if your meals are built right, but you're constantly grazing and constantly eating, your insulin stays high. Your fat burning never kicks in and never starts. But when you build in space, your metabolism picks up. your hormones level out and fat burning because something that your body does naturally. So now you've got the habits, you've got the rhythm. - Why Women Over 40 Need MORE Protein | Metabolism, Muscle & Craving Control: Kind: captions Language: en So, let's clear something up. Protein is a functional macronutrient, not a fuel source like carbs or fat. Yes, your body can break it down for energy in a pinch, but it does that at a cost, often by cannibalizing your own muscle tissue. So, let's not let it get to that point, okay? Instead, think of protein as your body's construction material, your internal repair crew. Your skin, bones, hair, enzymes, neurotransmitters, immune cells, they're all made from amino acids. These are the building blocks of protein. And here's something else most people don't realize. You don't store amino acids like you do carbohydrates as glycogen or fat. There's no protein pantry in your body. So when you're not getting in enough protein, your body has one place to go look for them, your muscle. And once you start losing muscle, your metabolism slows, your insulin sensitivity drops, and body fat starts accumulating. That is not aging powerfully. That is aging poorly. So here is how protein keeps you lean, strong, and energized. First of all, protein is what triggers muscle protein synthesis. This is that process of repairing and rebuilding muscle. Now, you've probably heard me talk about this that after the age of 30, we can lose up to 3 to 8% of our muscle mass per decade, even as much as 1% a year. And protein is your first line of defense. Plus, protein is very thermic. Protein takes more energy to digest and assimilate than fat or carbs. That means your body burns more calories just breaking it down. up to 25 to 30% of protein calories go towards digesting that protein. It's literally a metabolism booster. Also, it's amazing for satiety and craving control because protein is the most satiating macronutrient. It turns on fullness hormones like peptide Y and GLP1. You've heard about that one. And keeps grein, your hunger hormone, in check. What does that mean for you? fewer cravings, better appetite regulation, and way less snacking. So, it turns out your body has a builtin drive to meet its protein needs. If your body is too lower, your diet rather is too low in protein, it's going to keep you eating until you hit that threshold, even if it means overeating calories from carbs and fat. If you get your protein right, guess what? You naturally eat less. Period. Now, especially after 40, you need more protein, not less. So, if you're over 40, listen up. Because as we age or as we become more insulin resistant or more sedentary, we develop something called anabolic resistance. This is where our bodies become less efficient at building and maintaining muscle from the same amount of protein. So, while your younger self may have been fine with 20 grams per meal, your midlife body needs more like 30 to 40 grams per meal. and ideally spaced evenly throughout the day. Although overall protein is still the most important, you also got to factor in stress, gut health, and lowered stomach acid because all of these can impair protein digestion and absorption. This means that the quality of your protein, the digestibility of it, and the frequency you eat it become non-negotiable if you want to age powerfully. So, yes, protein helps you drop fat, but even more, it helps you preserve muscle, stabilize blood sugar, and feel full and satisfied while doing it. And that's what we're after, right? Fat loss without muscle loss. Because that's the real needle mover when it comes to long-term health, energy, and great body composition.

- Nutrition Tips for Women 40+: Energy & Metabolism: Kind: captions Language: en Nutrition matters after 40. A women over 40 experience natural changes in metabolism, muscle mass, and energy levels. Nutrition plays a key role in addressing these shifts. With the right foods, you can support your metabolism, maintain muscle, and power your workouts. Let's explore how to fuel your body for lasting vitality and health. Protein building blocks for muscle. Protein becomes increasingly important as we age. Consuming lean protein sources such as chicken, fish, eggs, tofu, and legumes helps preserve muscle mass, which naturally declines after 40. Aim to include a good protein source at every meal to support muscle repair and boost metabolism. [Music] Smart carbohydrates for energy. Carbohydrates are essential for energy, especially around exercise. Focus on complex carbs like whole grains, sweet potatoes, and beans, which provides sustained fuel and stabilize blood sugar. Avoiding excessive refined sugars helps prevent energy crashes, supporting both workouts and daily activities. Healthy fats supporting hormones and satiety. Including healthy fats supports hormone balance and keeps you satisfied. Foods like avocados, nuts, seeds, and olive oil offer anti-inflammatory benefits and promote heart health. Moderation is key as fats are calorie dense, but cutting them out completely can negatively affect energy and mood. Micronutrients and hydration. Don't overlook vitamins and minerals. Nutrients like calcium, vitamin D, magnesium, and iron are vital for bone health, energy production, and muscle function. Staying hydrated also aids metabolism and performance. Aim to drink water throughout the day, especially before and after exercise. Creating a sustainable nutrition plan for women over 40. Optimal nutrition means balance, variety, and consistency. Prioritize whole foods, mindful portions, and regular meal timing. Listen to your body's needs and partner nutrition with regular strength and aerobic activity for the best results in energy and metabolism.

- 10 Reasons Women Over 40 Need More Protein (It's Not Just Building Muscle): Kind: captions Language: en If you're working to lose fat as you build muscle, you may have heard to increase your protein intake, and you should. But eating more protein isn't just beneficial for body recmp. It's also good for your health and staying functionally fit till your final day on this planet. So, if you've been avoiding increasing your protein intake, I want to help you value this change and prioritize it by understanding fully why it's so key. Because we prioritize and embrace hard changes that we value. Here are 10 reasons why increasing your protein is so key. But before I dive into these amazing benefits of protein, I do want to give you one word of warning specifically about those high protein labeled foods you may turn to. Just because something says it's high protein doesn't mean it is. In the US, a food can be labeled as high protein with just 10 g of protein per serving. Which, don't get me wrong, that may be the boost you need while helping you strike a lifestyle balance. A high protein granola may help you strike balance in keeping your breakfast parfait while rocking your macro ratios. But you want to be aware that you may not actually be getting that much protein just by eating foods labeled high protein. Your best bet for increasing your protein are things like meat, chicken, beef, and seafood. Or if you're plant-based, tofu, tempe, and satan. However, if you're already increasing those protein portions and want other options, plus you're tempted by protein cereals or granas to help you strike a sustainable nutritional balance, you can use this trick to help you figure out the protein density of foods and ones that may be more valuable for your goals. Take the calories on the label and move the decimal over one to the left. If a cereal is 160 calories, that means the number you're now looking at is 16. If the protein on the nutritional label is 16 g or greater, that food is actually going to be protein dense under that amount and you know you're not looking at a protein dense food. Now, the 10 reasons to increase your protein. Number one, it helps build and maintain lean muscle mass. Grip strength is a key indicator of longevity. And guess what's needed for a strong grip? Muscle. Muscle is magical and involved in keeping our body healthy in so many ways, supporting everything from brain to bone health to metabolic health. So, we need to do everything we can to maintain and even build it. Especially since as we get older, our risk of sarcopenia or muscle loss increases and we're less able to utilize protein as efficiently to repair and rebuild. So, increasing our protein, the amino acids that are the building blocks of muscle, is really super key as we get older. Number two, it improves hormonal balance. Most people think of protein as just the building blocks of muscle, but it's also the building blocks of many hormones and neurotransmitters. Without enough protein, your body struggles to make and regulate the powerful chemical signals that tell your body when to burn fat, when to feel hungry or full, when to calm down, when to feel energized, and even when it's time to sleep. And especially as women enter pmenopause and menopause, protein is one of the most powerful dietary tools we have to feel more balanced, energized, and more in control of our body composition. Number three, protein keeps bones healthy. As we get older, our risk for falls and fractures increases. Protein helps us not only build muscle to react to avoid falls and slips that may impact our bone health, but it also provides amino acids to strengthen our bones while supporting calcium absorption. Add lifting weights to the mix and you create the perfect recipe for improving bone density and staying strong and stable. Number four, it improves mood and helps us manage stress. Again, protein isn't just for your muscles. It's also fuel for your brain and nervous system. The amino acids from protein are used to make key brain chemicals. Things like serotonin, which supports calm and happiness, dopamine, which helps with focus and motivation, and GABA, which plays a big role in relaxation and stress control. Together, these chemicals help keep your mood steady, your stress in check, and your energy more consistent. Protein also helps keep your blood sugar steady, which prevents the crashes that can trigger irritability, anxiety, and fatigue, which is especially helpful for anyone going through pmenopause or menopause. That's why making every meal protein centric is one of the most powerful ways to improve your mood, sharpen your focus, and increase your mental resilience as you get older. Number five, protein supports brain health and cognitive functioning. Protein does this both directly and indirectly. Indirectly, protein helps you build and maintain lean muscle mass. Strong muscles improve circulation, stabilize blood sugar, reduce inflammation, and are linked to a lower risk of dementia. Directly, protein fuels your brain by providing amino acids to make neurotransmitters like serotonin, dopamine, and acetyloline, supporting nerve protection and repair, keeping blood sugar steady for consistent focus and mental clarity, and reducing oxidative stress that damages brain cells. During menopause, these benefits become even more important. Protein helps protect against brain fog and memory lapses by supporting neurotransmitters, stabilizing blood sugar, and improving mental clarity and focus. By eating enough protein and keeping your muscle strong, you're not only supporting better mood, focus, and memory. Now, you're also investing in long-term brain health and lowering your risk of cognitive decline. Number six, it improves your cardiovascular health. You might be raising an eyebrow at this one. After all, when we think protein, many of us think of heart issues, especially with red meat and saturated fat. But here's the truth. Getting enough protein paired with heart-healthy fats actually supports your cardiovascular health. It helps lower blood pressure, improves cholesterol and triglyceride levels, and keeps arteries more flexible instead of stiffening with age. Protein also stabilizes blood sugar, reducing another major risk factor for heart disease. And let's not forget, protein builds muscle, and strong muscles directly benefit your heart by boosting circulation, blood sugar control, and lowering inflammation. All of this becomes even more important as women enter menopause. As estrogen levels decline, blood pressure often rises, cholesterol worsens, and blood sugar becomes harder to manage. All this puts extra strain on the heart and blood vessels. So, if you've been on the fence about protein, hopefully you're starting to see its benefits. Number seven, protein supports quality sleep and recovery. Want to fall asleep faster, sleep deeper, and reduce fatigue and muscle soreness? Yep, protein is key for all of these things. Protein provides amino acids like tryptophen, which your body uses to make serotonin, a calming neurotransmitter, and melatonin, the sleep hormone. This helps improve both your ability to fall asleep, but also how restorative your sleep actually is. Protein also helps stabilize blood sugar throughout the day, preventing the nighttime crashes and stress signals that often disrupt sleep. This is something that becomes especially important during menopause when sleep issues are more common. And when it comes to recovery, protein is literally the fuel your body needs to repair muscle tissue and rebuild stronger. Without enough of it, you can end up training hard but never seeing the muscle or strength gains you want and staying sore much longer than you should. Number eight, it improves insulin sensitivity and body composition. Notice where your storing fat has maybe changed with middle age that you're seeing your waistline spread a little bit more. Don't just write it off as getting older. Increase your protein intake. One of protein's biggest benefits is how it supports healthy blood sugar and insulin levels. When we eat enough protein, it slows digestion, reduces blood sugar spikes and crashes, and helps us feel fuller for longer, which means fewer cravings, and less overeating. Protein also supports lean muscle, and muscle is one of the body's best tools for managing blood sugar because it soaks up glucose and uses it for energy. Simply put, stronger muscles equal better insulin sensitivity. And while these benefits matter at any age, they become even more important during menopause when hormonal shifts can make it easier to store fat around the abdomen and harder to regulate blood sugar levels. Number nine, it keeps your gut healthy. Your gut does more than just digest food. It's also where nutrients are absorbed and where much of your immune system lives. As we age, digestive enzyme activity can decline, which makes it harder to break down and absorb nutrients as efficiently. This is where increasing protein can help. Certain amino acids like glutamine help maintain the integrity of your gut lining. The lining acts like a filter, letting in the nutrients your body needs while keeping out unwanted particles that can trigger inflammation. Protein also supports the production of digestive enzymes that help you break down food more effectively. And during menopause, when bloating and digestive discomfort often increase, protein helps support gut function and reduces inflammation so your digestion feels smoother and more balanced. And finally, even though I know you're already sold on increasing your protein. Number 10, protein strengthens your immune system. As we age, our immune system naturally slows down. This can make it harder to fight off infections, heal quickly, or keep inflammation under control. Protein directly supports your defenses by providing amino acids your body needs to build antibodies, immune cells, and glutathione, which is one of the most powerful antioxidants, protecting cells from damage. And because so much of your immune system actually lives in your gut, protein's role in maintaining your gut health further supports a strong balanced immune response. This is even more important during menopause when hormonal shifts can increase inflammation and make the immune system more vulnerable. So, increasing your protein intake, it isn't just key for your aesthetic goals, although it will help you look freaking fabulous. It's the key to staying fit, healthy, strong, and resilient till your final day on this planet. [Music]

My self-worth was directly tied to my weight since I was 12 years old. I made sure that you knew that I knew I was fat when you met me. Being self-deprecating was my way of being funny, but it also became a part of who I was. If anyone tried to take my picture, I would smile. But inside, I felt so embarrassed about my weight. My smile was not real. If you would have asked me whether or not I would have been fat on my wedding day, I would have said, "Of course not." I dreamed of how I would look on my wedding day, just like everybody else does. But the reality was, I did not lose the weight. I can look back now and accept that I was fat on my wedding day. But I remember feeling extremely disappointed in myself for not following through again. I've tried every diet or gimmick under the sun over the last 23 years to lose weight. I'll admit most of these attempts lasted anywhere from an hour to a couple of months. Regardless of how many times I dieted over the years, I continued to gain weight. Sometimes I would ask myself, "What if I never lose weight? What if I die never experiencing anything other than being obese and out of shape? I've tried keto countless times in the past and I always went back to eating carbs. I know now that I was and am a carb addict. But my twin sister's wedding was motivation for me to try keto again. She was determined to lose as much weight as possible and I didn't want to be obviously bigger than her on her wedding day. So, I started keto again on January 19th, 2019. But this time, I stuck to it. The weight came off slowly compared to how fast I lost weight in the past, but I kept going. I made hard decisions. I said no about a thousand times. My cravings for carbs and sugar started to fade. I learned to cook amazing low carb meals from scratch. I found myself losing anywhere from a pound to even 2 lbs a week. And by the time spring rolled around, I felt motivated to start up old hobbies. I spent time outside for the first time in years. I grew veggies and beautiful flowers in my backyard. I noticed I no longer got hot outside, even when it was 90°. My life was changing. By the time my sister's wedding came around, I weighed in at 189 lb. [Applause] I felt the most confident I ever had before. While I used to hide in pictures, I found myself enjoying having my picture taken at Sarah's wedding. Throughout my weight loss journey, there were several weeks that I lost nothing, but overall, my weight continued to go down. I went from a size extra large to a size small. When I look at myself now, I still can't believe what I'm seeing most of the time. I can actually fit in clothes now and shopping is fun. All I know is for the first time in my life, I feel good about myself. Sure, I still have flaws just like everybody else does, and I still would like to lose another 20 lb, but it's all gravy from here. My starting weight was 262 lb, and today I weigh in at 168 lb. I am no longer obese. My weight is considered normal for the first time in my life. If you're struggling with weight loss right now, just know that if I can do it, anybody can. Just take it one day at a time, one meal at a time. Keep on going. Don't focus on how long it's going to take you to lose the weight. You have to focus on how far you've come already. Momentum builds momentum. Time is going to pass whether you're on this diet or not. But if you stay true to the diet, one day you're going to wake up and you're going to be so far into your ketogenic journey that you're not going to be able to go back to the way that you were living your life before. And the next time someone asks you to smile for a picture, it will be for real.

more than a hundred years ago to stabilize brain chemistry in children with severe seizures um and this was long before the availability of useful seizure medications so these were children who um uh were having multiple seizures per day in many cases and so the ketogenic diet was designed back then created back then a very strict version of the ketogenic diet um to get as close as possible to to   4. @葉瓊鞠 [4 likes]: 一、講座核心內容總結
1. 飲食與腦部代謝的關係
Dr. Ede 強調現代飲食（高糖、高精製碳水、超加工食品）會引發慢性發炎、氧化壓力與胰島素抗性，這些都是導致抑鬱、焦慮、多型鬱躁症等精神狀態的幕後因素 

2. 低碳酮體作為腦部穩定燃料
在飲食導致胰島素升高、細胞阻抗時，腦部能量供應障礙，導致思維不清晰、情緒波動。採用酮體為主燃料的飲食（即生酮飲食）能提供更穩定、清潔的能量來源，有助於提高腦部穩定性與情緒控制 
3. 精神疾病與癲癇之間的相關性
她提到生酮飲食長久以來用於控制癲癇（尤其難治型），同時許多心理健康疾病（如躁鬱症）也涉及神經穩定性失衡。這些類似性使生酮飲食成為一種腦部穩定飲食治療策略 

4. 個人化飲食策略的重要性
並非每個人都適用同一種飲食方式。Dr. Ede 建議從「Quiet Paleo」開始（低敏、低碳、不加調味），若無效再進階至「Quiet Keto」，最後可能演變為「Quiet Carnivore」。透過逐步 reintroduce 食物，找到最適個人化飲食模

5. 飲食對成癮與暴食的潛在幫助
許多案例指出，採生酮或類肉食飲食後，暴食、酒精渴望或賭博衝動等行為顯著改善。這類飲食可協助穩定血糖與情緒，有助於減少衝動性

二、書中重點概念
來自《Change Your Diet, Change Your Mind》一書與講座一致的核心概念包括：

項目	要點說明
胰島素抗性與所謂「腦部第3型糖尿病」	胰島素阻抗時，腦無法有效利用葡萄糖，導致能量短缺與情緒失調 
厭惡植物食物理論	部分植物含抗營養物質（如草酸、苯甲酸、植酸等），可能加重腦部／腸道敏感者的症狀 
Dr. Ronald Hoffman |
營養完整性與動物性食物	建議攝取高品質動物蛋白質、脂肪與適量低碳蔬菜以確保長期腦部營養健康 

三、臨床與實踐建議
若正在考慮飲食介入常見精神症狀（如焦慮、情緒不穩、躁鬱傾向等），建議先諮詢熟悉代謝精神醫學或生酮療法的臨床醫師。

檢測胰島素、空腹血糖、裡脂比、炎症指標等代謝指標，有助於評估生酮飲食是否適合個人情況。

生酮並非一勞永逸，亦需搭配飲食多樣性管理、電解質補充、個人化追蹤。

四、總結
Dr. Ede 的講座與著作共同強調：

飲食是影響腦部能量、神經傳導與情緒穩定的關鍵。

生酮飲食可作為精神疾病的輔助治療策略，特別對於難以控制的情緒或衝動症狀有潛在益處。

強調從簡到嚴漸進個人化飲食調整，並非所有人都適合走到最嚴格的Carnivore。


資料來源
你說：
Quiet Keto 飲食、實際餐單範例

好的！我整理一下 Dr. Georgia Ede 所提到的 Quiet Keto 飲食概念，並附上幾份實際餐單範例，讓你能更清楚了解。

一、Quiet Keto 飲食核心概念
Quiet Keto 是 Ede 博士提出的低刺激、低敏感版本的生酮飲食，目標是：

維持酮症，提供腦部穩定能量

減少潛在發炎與食物過敏原

降低消化與精神負擔

基本原則
極低碳水

碳水攝取量通常低於每日 20 克淨碳水

移除高糖、精製澱粉、水果（少量莓果可後期視情況加回）

中高脂肪

來自天然動物脂肪（牛油、豬油、雞油、鴨油、魚油）、初榨橄欖油、牛油果油等

中等蛋白質

以肉類、魚類、蛋類為主

限制或暫停高敏蔬菜

暫時減少茄科（番茄、茄子、辣椒）、高草酸蔬菜（菠菜、甜菜葉）

常選用低刺激蔬菜（西葫蘆、花椰菜、白菜等）

不使用加工調味料

降低食品添加物與潛在過敏源

逐步重新引入食材

當症狀改善後，可慢慢測試蔬菜、香料、乳製品等耐受度

二、Quiet Keto 實際餐單範例
以下餐單以每日 2～3 餐為範例，可視個人作息調整。

範例 1（初期適應期）
早餐

雞蛋 2 顆，用草飼牛油煎

牛油果半顆

黑咖啡（可加 MCT 油或鮮奶油，若耐受乳製品）

午餐

鹽煎牛小排（油脂不去掉）

清燙四季豆（少量橄欖油拌）

鹽、胡椒簡單調味

晚餐

烤鮭魚（附魚皮）

花椰菜泥（用奶油與鹽調味）

西葫蘆絲拌橄欖油

範例 2（穩定期，可增加食材變化）
早餐

培根 2 片 + 半熟蛋 2 顆

牛油果 1/4 顆

黑咖啡或草本茶

午餐

烤雞腿（保留雞皮）

白花椰菜＋奶油

橄欖油淋少許檸檬汁

晚餐

烤鯖魚或沙丁魚罐頭（橄欖油浸）

青江菜或白菜

少量牛油提香

範例 3（進入長期維持）
早餐（可省略，進行間歇性斷食）

黑咖啡 / 草本茶

MCT 油或牛油咖啡（若適合）

午餐

羊排 200g

烤蘆筍（橄欖油拌）

奶油花椰菜泥

晚餐

烤牛肋條

西葫蘆與香菇（橄欖油炒）

鹽、胡椒調味

三、Quiet Keto 的補充注意事項
電解質補充：生酮初期容易流失鈉、鉀、鎂，可透過鹽水、骨湯或補充劑補足

水分攝取：每日足夠水分，避免脫水與酮流感

追蹤反應：觀察情緒、睡眠、腸胃及能量變化

逐步調整：如症狀穩定，可慢慢測試加入香料、不同蔬菜甚至乳製品

  1. @jessicafruehauf2241 [97 likes]: After giving birth to my first child 🍼👶  about ten years ago, I joined a popular weight loss group in my area. At first, I thought it was amazing – you could eat as much food from an allowed list as you like, and you could still have treats like crisps and chocolate as long as you counted them.
 I lost a lot of weight quite quickly but got to a point where I just couldn’t stick to this plan anymore. I had an uncontrollable urge to eat everything that wasn’t allowed on the diet plan. Ultimately, I just ended up binge eating everything I wasn’t supposed to be eating. At this point, my mental health had taken a nosedive. I suffered from terrible social anxiety and was at a complete loss.I saw one of my online friends was having great success with a ketogenic way of eating. It sparked my interest, and I ended up doing extensive research and came across an article about keto and decided to try it .Within a few weeks, my mental health was so much better. My head felt clearer, and my anxiety was decreasing. But more importantly, the constant urge to binge has gone. I finally felt like my life was not revolving around food.

  7. @MsK-pt4kc: So many like Dr. Fung and Ted Neiman say it's not about calories, but how your body reacts to the food you eat. We know that our body responds differently to 100 calories of broccoli as opposed to 100 calories of cake. Also the response to the thermodynamic of food, calories in calories out assumes that we are all machines and that we all respond the same and that is simply not true.  Yes some people can lose on a higher carb lower calorie diet (usually younger people and those who haven't yo-yo dieted for so many years). Still, it's usually not sustainable. If it was, Weight Watchers would be out of business. But some people have ruined their metabolisms and have dieted for so many years that they have to go super low carb even keto to fix their metabolism and control insulin which is the key to weight loss, keeping insulin lowest possible by eating low carb and perhaps fasting to also increase grelin. They also can't just eat a tiny bit of carbs because they are trigger foods. Having one bite of carbs can cause cravings for more and it's too hard to stop. Also carbs are highly inflammatory and many people are allergic to wheat and most grains. Eating low-carb and Keto will naturally cause you to eat less but you don't have to watch your calories. You can put somebody on a 500 calorie daily diet and then give them insulin and they will gain weight. But if you eat lower carb, abstain from eating all day then you will be successful. But yes whatever works for you do it, my argument is that it's not all about calories, calories do count but they are not the end all be all

We are in a hurry all the time to get to some place. And we're indoctrinated into that at a very young age. From the beginnings of school where we're chasing grades and, you know, trying to get to the next level. there's always some sort of goal that we're chasing or some gold star that we're grabbing for and we're measuring our self-worth against the extent to which we're keeping up with our peers. And we carry it through our entire life putting pressure on ourselves, always believing that we're behind and we have to we have to catch up or we're just never going to get like quote unquote there. And I think that's a real violence that we do, this socially imposed violence. And in my experience, yes, life is short. We have one precious life. We should all, you know, devote ourselves to making the most of it. But life is also long. And we're not in a race. That people can become addicted to lots of different things, whether it's to busyness, to drugs, to video games, to toxic relationships, to chaos in their lives. Can you talk a little bit more about that just to widen the spectrum? >> I've been uh in recovery for a long time. I've seen people get sober. I've seen people die. And what I've taken of the many things I've taken from this experience is is a conviction that addiction is very much a spectrum disease. And it's a disease in which we put all this focus on the behavior or the substance. Alcohol, drugs, gambling, sex and love. But these are not the problem. These are the solution to the problem. When you remove alcohol from an alcoholic's life, you're basically forcing that person to break up with their best friend, their reliable coping strategy for life. And what you're left with is a raw nerve of a human being that then has to be healed. And I think we have traditionally thought of addiction in the context of these these behaviors and substances. U but I think that's a very restrictive definition. I think to some degree or another on this spectrum, we can all relate to behaviors that we feel powerless to change or overcome or compulsions we know are leading us astray and yet we keep doing it and doing it despite wreaking havoc on our lives. So, how can you spot a lifestyle creep and what should we do about it? Just talk to us a little bit about this. Yeah. So, if you find yourself like making more money than you've ever made, but you still are in the same place financially, nine times out of 10, it's due to lifestyle creep. Because again, if you're used to spending everything you make, making more money isn't going to change that. You're just going to expand to the new a new level of spending. >> So, that is what's happening. So, that's an awareness point. If you're thinking to yourself like, "Oh my gosh, last year or let's just say over the last two or three years, I'm making more than I've ever made yet. I still find myself myself stuck in the cycle of credit card debt or I don't have as much saved. I'm not investing like I want to, then it's very likely due to lifestyle creep." So, that's a level of awareness that you need to have. So, the first step to changing anything is just awareness. Like, oh, this is why you're probably um living in a more expensive house, maybe driving in a more expensive car, maybe you're doing more activities because you can, because you can afford it, all the things. - Reducing Belly Fat with a Low-Carb Polyphenol-Rich Diet: Kind: captions Language: en-US Deep belly fat, or visceral fat, that is&nbsp; stored around the abdominal organs is linked&nbsp;&nbsp; to significant health risks, including heart&nbsp; disease, type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers. There are proven strategies&nbsp; for reducing belly fat. One&nbsp;&nbsp; that I have covered extensively&nbsp; on my channel is a low-carb diet. Another strategy is to boost&nbsp; your intake of polyphenols. But here is the thing: Polyphenols come&nbsp; from plants, and because plants make&nbsp;&nbsp; carbohydrates through photosynthesis, boosting&nbsp; polyphenols means you boost your carb intake. Is there a way to marry these two strategies?&nbsp; In other words, can you enjoy your polyphenols&nbsp;&nbsp; and have your low-carb diet benefits, too?&nbsp; Absolutely, and not only is it enjoyable&nbsp;&nbsp; because of the great foods you get to eat,&nbsp; it is also easy to follow and effective. This video shares how polyphenols help to reduce&nbsp;&nbsp; belly fat and the low-carb&nbsp; foods that contain them. [Intro Music] Before I start, let me take one minute&nbsp; to thank LMNT for sponsoring this video. Low-carb diets work for fat loss because they&nbsp; keep the fat-storing hormone insulin low.&nbsp; When insulin is low, you also lose sodium more&nbsp;&nbsp; rapidly. That is why people who eat a&nbsp; whole-food, low-carb diet need salt. We have been taught to avoid salt or sodium.&nbsp; However, sodium is an essential nutrient,&nbsp;&nbsp; meaning your body can't make it, so it must&nbsp; come in through your diet. Sodium is needed&nbsp;&nbsp; to conduct nerve impulses, contract and relax&nbsp; muscles, and maintain proper hydration. When it&nbsp;&nbsp; is low, it can lead to fatigue, muscle&nbsp; cramps, headaches, and irritability. You can get sodium from low-carb&nbsp; foods like pickles, olives,&nbsp;&nbsp; or just the salt shaker. Or you can&nbsp; do what I do and use LMNT every day. LMNT not only tastes great but also&nbsp; contains magnesium and potassium,&nbsp;&nbsp; two additional essential nutrients needed&nbsp; for proper nerve and muscle function. When you use my link, drinklmnt.com/drbecky,&nbsp; to make any purchase, you will receive a free&nbsp;&nbsp; sample pack with eight great flavors. I will&nbsp; leave a link in the description area below. Unlike subcutaneous fat that you can&nbsp; pinch under your skin, the deeper visceral&nbsp;&nbsp; fat is metabolically active and secretes&nbsp; pro-inflammatory cytokines and adipokines. The resulting low-grade, chronic inflammation&nbsp; contributes to insulin resistance,&nbsp;&nbsp; a condition where cells in the body&nbsp; become less responsive to insulin.&nbsp; This resistance impairs glucose&nbsp; uptake, leading to elevated blood&nbsp;&nbsp; sugar levels and promoting fat storage,&nbsp; particularly in the abdominal region. So, you see a vicious cycle emerge&nbsp; where the accumulation of belly fat&nbsp;&nbsp; releases substances that exacerbate inflammation.&nbsp;&nbsp; That inflammation contributes to insulin&nbsp; resistance, which promotes fat storage. Polyphenols are known for their&nbsp; antioxidant properties, so they have&nbsp;&nbsp; powerful anti-inflammatory effects that break&nbsp; that vicious cycle and help reduce belly fat. They also improve metabolic&nbsp; health by enhancing fat oxidation,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is the process of breaking down&nbsp; fatty acids for energy. This enhanced&nbsp;&nbsp; fat breakdown leads to improved energy&nbsp; expenditure and reduced fat storage. Polyphenol-rich foods also suppress&nbsp; appetite by inhibiting the secretion&nbsp;&nbsp; of appetite-stimulating hormones, like&nbsp; ghrelin, and increasing adiponectin levels,&nbsp;&nbsp; furthering this hunger satisfaction effect&nbsp; thanks to the influence adiponectin has on&nbsp;&nbsp; chemical messengers in the hypothalamus&nbsp; that regulate hunger and satiety. Adiponectin also improves insulin sensitivity,&nbsp;&nbsp; allowing cells to better utilize&nbsp; glucose for energy. This reduces&nbsp;&nbsp; blood sugar levels and decreases the storage&nbsp; of fat, particularly in the abdominal area. So, there are a lot of wins when it comes&nbsp; to reducing belly fat by eating polyphenols.&nbsp;&nbsp; And best yet, the foods taste good, and&nbsp; many are naturally low in carbohydrates. Now, to this point in the video,&nbsp; I have talked about polyphenols,&nbsp;&nbsp; which sounds like a single&nbsp; substance found in food. Actually,&nbsp;&nbsp; the term polyphenol is a general term&nbsp; used to describe a class of compounds. There are literally thousands of&nbsp; them, so to reap the full benefits,&nbsp;&nbsp; it is best to incorporate a variety of&nbsp; polyphenol-rich foods into your diet.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let's look at some great low-carb&nbsp; sources, starting with berries. Not all fruits are appropriate for low-carb&nbsp; dieters, but berries, in general, are OK,&nbsp;&nbsp; and their vibrant colors tell&nbsp; us they are rich in polyphenols. We have all heard it said that if you want to ward&nbsp; off illness, you should eat a rainbow every day,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is a catchy way of reminding us to eat&nbsp; a variety of colorful fruits and vegetables.&nbsp;&nbsp; What you are really being told to do is&nbsp; eat your polyphenols every day because&nbsp;&nbsp; many of those rich colors come from these&nbsp; naturally occurring chemical compounds. There are differences among berries. Strawberries&nbsp; are a good low-carb choice because they are widely&nbsp;&nbsp; liked, and a 3.5-ounce (100g) serving contains&nbsp; just 7.7 grams of carbs and 2 grams of fiber.&nbsp;&nbsp; You can also choose blackberries or&nbsp; raspberries. Blueberries are a bit&nbsp;&nbsp; more middle of the range when&nbsp; it comes to their carbohydrate&nbsp;&nbsp; content. We see that a serving of&nbsp; blueberries has 14.5g of carbs. Other fruits high in polyphenols that can be&nbsp; worked into a low-carb diet include plums and&nbsp;&nbsp; apples. You will also find a rich supply&nbsp; of polyphenols in cherries, pomegranates,&nbsp;&nbsp; and grapes, but these fruits have higher&nbsp; carb counts. For the full picture on fruits,&nbsp;&nbsp; see my video on the best and&nbsp; worst low-carb fruit options. Vegetables rich in polyphenols include&nbsp; spinach, broccoli, Brussels sprouts,&nbsp;&nbsp; red onions, asparagus, and artichokes.&nbsp; These are all non-starchy vegetables,&nbsp;&nbsp; so they fit easily into a low-carb diet. To give you an idea, this entire bag of&nbsp; spinach contains just 5 carbohydrates,&nbsp;&nbsp; and you could cook and eat half of this one-pound&nbsp; bag of broccoli and only consume 10 carbs. Carrots are high in polyphenols, which&nbsp; you might guess because of their rich&nbsp;&nbsp; orange color. However, they ride the&nbsp; fence between starchy and non-starchy,&nbsp;&nbsp; so if your goal is to keep your carbs&nbsp; low, limit your intake of carrots. Nuts and seeds are a tasty way to add polyphenols&nbsp; to your daily low-carb diet. The best choices for&nbsp;&nbsp; their polyphenol count are pecans, walnuts,&nbsp; hazelnuts, almonds, flaxseeds, and chia seeds. These foods also provide a great&nbsp; mix of healthy fats and protein,&nbsp;&nbsp; and while being relatively low in&nbsp; carbs, they are easy to overeat. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; do not think of them as a snack food;&nbsp; think of them as a recipe enhancer,&nbsp;&nbsp; so sprinkle chopped pecans, walnuts, or slivered&nbsp; almonds on top of a salad or mix them into&nbsp;&nbsp; full-fat yogurt. You can also add chia seeds&nbsp; and flaxseed to yogurt or a low-carb smoothie. That kind of segways us into beverages.&nbsp; You can drink polyphenols because they&nbsp;&nbsp; are abundant in green tea, coffee, and also&nbsp; red wine. You have heard that the healthy&nbsp;&nbsp; component of red wine is resveratrol.&nbsp; Resveratrol is a type of polyphenol. Other foods that are high in polyphenols&nbsp; and low in carbohydrates are olives,&nbsp;&nbsp; which can be eaten as a snack or&nbsp; included in recipes, and dark chocolate. However, when it comes to dark chocolate,&nbsp;&nbsp; you need to choose wisely. The chocolate&nbsp; needs to be at least 70 percent cacao,&nbsp;&nbsp; preferably 80 percent and above, and it should not&nbsp; have sugar listed in the top three ingredients. That by itself takes some searching,&nbsp; but it is also best to find brands low&nbsp;&nbsp; in lead and cadmium. I will link&nbsp; to the Consumer Reports website&nbsp;&nbsp; where the safer chocolates and those&nbsp; highest in heavy metals are listed. And, when you are preparing food at home,&nbsp; you can give the recipe a polyphenol boost&nbsp;&nbsp; by using olive oil or sprinkling in herbs and&nbsp; spices. Several of them are high in polyphenols,&nbsp;&nbsp; including oregano, thyme, rosemary, basil,&nbsp; parsley, cilantro, cinnamon, and turmeric. And, just to be complete, I will mention that&nbsp; there are a few high-carb foods that are rich in&nbsp;&nbsp; polyphenols. They include grains like oats, rye,&nbsp; barley, and wheat. Beans are another high-carb&nbsp;&nbsp; food with polyphenols, with black beans and&nbsp; red kidney beans having the highest levels. Polyphenols support your goal to lose&nbsp; belly fat by reducing inflammation,&nbsp;&nbsp; enhancing fat oxidation, suppressing&nbsp; appetite, and improving insulin sensitivity. These natural substances are abundant in&nbsp; many low-carb foods, including berries,&nbsp;&nbsp; non-starchy vegetables, nuts and seeds, dark&nbsp; chocolate, olives, olive oil, and herbs. You&nbsp;&nbsp; can also drink polyphenols since they are&nbsp; abundant in green tea, coffee, or red wine. There are thousands of polyphenols&nbsp; that you can take in, so aim to&nbsp;&nbsp; get a variety of these foods into your&nbsp; daily diet for maximum health benefits. Thanks for&nbsp; watching and have a great rest of your day.

- 6 Healthy High Fat Foods That are Low Carb: Kind: captions Language: en we used to think that including fat in your diet was a bad idea but thanks to the popularity success and research behind low carb high fat diets dietary fats are making a comeback in this video I share six hunger stopping healthy high-fat foods as well as suggestions on how to work them into your everyday first on our list is the egg an egg is a perfect food and it is also a perfect example of a high-fat food that was once demonized the bad reputation came from the fact that the yolk of an egg is high in cholesterol however the cholesterol that we take in when we eat eggs doesn't affect the cholesterol in the blood a three-month study that compared a group of people who ate 12 eggs a week to a group that ate less than two eggs a week found no between-group differences in total cholesterol LDL cholesterol or triglycerides eggs give you a great mix of fat and protein which is a great combination for keeping hunger away they are also high in choline which is an important nutrient for many functions when it is deficient it is closely linked to the development of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and we also know that choline is a methyl donor which means that it can give its methyl group to another molecule to enhance or inhibit its action and you want methyl donors because they drive a lot of processes associated with metabolism heart health and the elimination of toxins from your body that could otherwise build up and cause disease and eggs are traditional breakfast foods but they work great for dinner for instance quiche is a great low carb choice at dinner time I have to put avocados next because they are right up there with eggs as far as health value I have a half of an avocado a day as a salad topper a half of this avocado weighs about 70 grams and contains about 10 grams of fat most of which are monounsaturated fats if improving your cholesterol profile is a goal avocados are a great thing to eat a meta-analysis which is a type of study analyzes the results of past research found that diets that regularly contain avocados significantly decrease cholesterol LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and a fiber is something that you are trying to increase in your low carb diet half of an avocado that's this size will give you four point six grams of fiber and only five point nine grams of carbs avocados also contain more potassium than bananas and potassium has a lot of roles in your body that help with fluid balance nerve impulses muscle contractions but this nutrient is regularly flushed out of your body so you need to replenish it if you are on a low-carb diet and experiencing muscle cramps potassium is an electrolyte that you want to replenish so eat an avocado or use an electrolyte supplement like the indoor electrolyte supplement that I often mention some types of fish are high in a healthy fat called omega-3 fatty acids omega-3s are special texture without the fishy smell that can turn some people off nuts can also be a good source of omega-3s with walnuts being one of the highest in that particular fat however unlike fish walnuts contain a different type of omega-3 called ala this omega-3 fatty acid may comes from plant foods and is arguably a less valuable form because to benefit from it your body must convert it into EPA and DHA so you can get omega-3s from plant foods but when they come in they need to go through a conversion step before you can get all of the health benefits from them and unfortunately many of us are inefficient at converting ala into the active forms walnuts do have a hidden advantage however because they are commonly eaten raw and I call that a hidden advantage because when nuts are roasted they are typically roasted in unhealthy vegetable oils that degrade with heat and promote inflammation in your body walnuts have a nice mix of fat protein and fiber which are all things that help keep hunger away and you can work walnuts into your daily diet they go great as a salad topper and because they have a mild flavor you can crush them up and hide them into recipes or make a crumble topping for cooked vegetables or fish chia seeds are another plant food that is high in the ala form of omega-3 fatty acids they are also a great source of fiber with 1 tablespoon containing about 4.2 grams of carbs 3.9 grams of which are fiber and only 49 calories so if you are looking for natural ways to increase your fiber in your low-carb diet chia seeds are a great choice but I would say that one of the most interesting things about chia seeds is how they absorb liquid to form a gel I added about a quarter cup of water to two tablespoons of chia seeds about 10 minutes ago and this is the speed at which it thickens this is going to slow digestion and help you feel full longer so I encourage you to experiment with chia seeds they have a mild almost undetectable flavor so you can add a spoonful to a smoothie or make a chia seed pudding or mix them into a full fat yogurt which takes two already healthy high-fat foods and combines them together I will sometime add a tablespoon of chia seeds to yogurt with berries to boost the fiber and nutrient value of the yogurt but full fat yogurt by itself is a great high-fat food so let's talk about this you can use yogurt for breakfast you can use it for a snack you can use it for dessert and full fat yogurt is a good source of fat and if you buy a brand that contains live cultures you get a whole host of probiotic bacteria that improve the health weight so any change in your diet that will promote the health of the gut microbiome is worthwhile now this is Greek yogurt regular yogurt is fine but Greek yogurt is strained during processing which lowers the lactose content so if you are lactose intolerant Greek yogurt would be the better choice and the things that you want to look for when picking yogurt are 4 to 5 percent milk fat live cultures and no added sugar so we have high fat low carb foods here that work from breakfast to snacks to dinner to dessert each of these foods is easy to prepare it's delicious and filled with   2. @Bithi-j6d: the thing people always overlook with high protein eating for weight loss is that not all protein sources do the same job, even at equal gram counts. like 30g of protein from chicken breast and 30g from a processed protein bar technically look the same on a tracker but your body handles them very differently in terms of satiety, thermic effect, and how long until hunger creeps back.

what genuinely surprised me when i started paying more attention to this: cottage cheese and eggs became my most used foods not because they're exciting but because the hunger suppression is noticeably longer than almost anything else i tried at similar calories. greek yogurt is close but i found the fat-free versions didn't hold me as long, the full-fat versions do something different with satiety that the numbers don't fully explain.

the point about fish is worth emphasizing too because people default to chicken breast as the one protein food and kind of forget that salmon and sardines are bringing omega-3s alongside the protein, which matters for inflammation and actually supports the metabolic side of weight loss in ways that go beyond just macros. i've been on compounded tirzepatide through formblends for about five months and one thing i've noticed is that building meals around these specific protein sources made a real difference in how smoothly the appetite suppression works, like it compounds the effect rather than fighting it.

the practical takeaway i'd add for anyone watching: aim to hit at least 25g protein at breakfast specifically, because that single change probably does more for your afternoon cravings than any other single adjustment you can make.

- 10 Low Carb Metabolism Boosting Foods for Thyroid Support: Kind: captions Language: en thyroid hormones regulate your metabolic rate to optimize their performance you need to get the right nutrients inside of you in this video i'll share 10 low carb foods that contain these nutrients so you can eat your way to a better metabolism every cell in your body needs thyroid hormones to help generate and use energy which ultimately regulates your basal metabolic rate or bmr to do their job these metabolic hormones must get inside of your cells which is no small feat because there are three obstacles your body must overcome first your thyroid gland must make a sufficient amount of hormones next the inactive form which is t4 must be converted to the active form which is t3 and then that activated hormone must cross the cell membrane to get inside your cell where it does its job overcoming each of these obstacles requires specific nutrients you can get those nutrients into your diet with these 10 foods first on our list is shrimp i put it first because it is rich in iodine which is one of the principal nutrients needed to make thyroid hormones in fact the full name for the active t3 is triodo thyronine and if you look closely at that name you'll see that the beginning of the word tri-iodo literally means three iodine atoms are within the chemical compound the problem is that our bodies cannot make iodine so we need to get it from our diets we do that by eating iodine rich foods like shrimp or other foods that come from the sea like other kinds of seafood or even seaweed for instance iodized salt is another option for boosting the amount of iodine that you're consuming however shrimp gives you some added thyroid benefits besides iodine it has a good source of selenium which is a mineral that helps to turn the inactive form of thyroid hormones which is that t4 into the active t3 form wild salmon is a type of fish that contains many nutrients that are vital for thyroid function one of those nutrients is tyrosine which is an amino acid like iodine tyrosine is needed for your thyroid gland to make thyroid hormones salmon also provides a healthy dose of selenium which i mentioned helps with the activation of thyroid hormones and it also is rich in omega-3 fatty acids these fatty acids support the integrity of your cell membranes creating a smooth path for thyroid hormones to cross into your cells where they regulate energy production so when you include salmon in your diet you provide the nutrients needed to make activate and deliver thyroid hormones more efficiently spinach and other dark leafy greens are a great source of selenium they also have iron and zinc iron and zinc work alongside selenium to enhance the conversion of t4 to t3 zinc also supports your immune system which may offer additional support for individuals with the most common form of hypothyroidism which is an autoimmune form called hashimoto's thyroiditis spinach can be used as a base of your daily salad or it can be incorporated in a recipe or omelettes or soups and these richly colored leaves also pack a healthy serving of vitamin a which is helpful for optimizing thyroid hormone function bone broth is rich in collagen and the gut healthy amino acids glycine and proline that help to prevent or heal leaky gut a leaky gut also called increased intestinal permeability is a condition that can increase your risk of the autoimmune form of hypothyroidism with leaky gut the cell junctions that line the digestive tract wall spread apart allowing small food particles to leak out of the tract and into the blood these leaked particles provide a path for food proteins like gluten and dairy proteins as well as other digested particles to pass into your bloodstream where your immune system identifies them as foreign invaders that must be attacked because gluten and dairy molecules look a lot like thyroid cells your thyroid cells get attacked in a case of mistaken identity this is a phenomenon known as molecular mimicry and has been identified as playing a role in autoimmune diseases there are different bone broths available in liquid or powder form that easily mixes with any hot or cold drink and the added perk of bone broth is the collagen that it has supports the health of your hair skin and nails but you do need to buy quality the cheap bone broth cartons that you find in the grocery store are likely only simmered for a few hours which is not long enough to extract the collagen and the nutrients that you need to benefit your gut and ultimately your thyroid garlic is often touted for its health benefits and contains nutrients beneficial for optimal thyroid function it is a good source of selenium and it might help your body to metabolize iron a daily dose of garlic will also supply your body with b vitamins needed for the support of your immune system and help to inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines that are involved in hashimoto's thyroiditis walnuts are a great source of plant-based omega-3 fatty acids these healthy fats support your cell membranes allowing thyroid hormones to move into your cells easily walnuts are something that i eat just about every day on my daily salad they provide a nice crunch they have a wonderful mix of macronutrients for low carb dieters even if you just have four or five walnut halves a day which is about a half of an ounce you'll get more than nine grams of healthy fats and less than two grams of carbs and while that half ounce will have 90 calories walnuts are hunger satiating making them an easy enjoyable and effective part of your overall weight loss plan now one small note according to the mayo clinic if you are taking thyroid hormone replacement medication walnuts may impair the absorption of the medication so they should not be consumed together okay next we have blueberries which are filled with the vitamins your body needs to make thyroid hormones and move your hormones into your cells and their deep rich color indicates that they are rich in antioxidants which are compounds that indirectly support thyroid health by reducing inflammation and boosting your immune system now blueberries are not the lowest carb berry but they have a favorable glycemic index of 53 so you can feel good about incorporating them into recipes without fear of a blood sugar spike that can block fat loss and trigger hunger brazil nuts are one of the most concentrated dietary sources of selenium and with only about three grams of carbs per ounce they are a satisfying way to support your thyroid function and brazil nuts as you can see are large in size and just one nut a day will be enough to meet or exceed the recommended dietary allowance for selenium which for adults is 55 micrograms coconut oil contains medium chain triglycerides or mcts that are easily metabolized to provide energy and encourage weight loss and one of the main benefits for thyroid support is the high concentration of lauric acid in coconut oil this particular mct prevents the overgrowth of gut bacteria viruses and fungi that can open the door to thyroid issues some people will stir coconut oil into their coffee but being that it remains stable when heated it is also a very good choice for cooking all right next on my list is one of my favorites which is pumpkin seeds these low carb seeds are a source of tyrosine which is the amino acid that links with iodine to make thyroid hormones being that many sources of tyrosine are animal based foods pumpkin seeds are a viable way for those who don't eat a lot of animal products to boost their tyrosine intake pumpkin seeds also supply iron and selenium which are two of the nutrients needed to convert inactive thyroid hormone to the active form you know when your thyroid hormones are doing their job your metabolism is working for you helping you burn calories and lose weight when you include these 10 low carb foods in your diet you give your body the nutrients it needs to support your thyroid and metabolism and you add to the enjoyment of your low-carb diet i put these foods together in a download for you that you can get from my website for free and i will share the links here on the video and below in the description please join my channel by clicking on the subscribe button and then the bell icon which will alert you when my next video is released thanks so much for watching

  13. @SeniorHealth60Channel: A really refreshing take on this topic. So much of the conversation around metabolism gets stuck on the simple 'eat less, move more' mantra, which can feel like fighting a battle against your own body. Your focus on supporting a foundational system like the thyroid is a far more nuanced and empowering approach.

This leads to a deeper philosophical question I'm always exploring: Is the ultimate goal to constantly 'manage' and 'boost' our metabolism with specific inputs, or is it to create an environment (through diet, lifestyle, even mindset) where the body can find its own natural, optimal equilibrium without our constant intervention?

Essentially, are we meant to be micromanagers of our biology, or gardeners tending to the soil so it can flourish on its own? I'd love to hear what others think about this distinction.
